cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. TensorFlow is vulnerable to a heap OOB write in `Grappler`. The `set_output` function writes to an array at the specified index. Hence, this gives a malicious user a write primitive. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: ctx->set_output(output_port, shape) is called with an unchecked output_port value, allowing out-of-bounds write into the internal outputs_ array.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash, or potential memory corruption if output_port is attacker-controlled (e.g., negative value or value exceeding the number of outputs for the node).

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled int, can be negative or >= num_outputs

step_by_step_execution.description: GetUnknownOutputShape is called with the potentially out-of-bounds output_port
GetContext(node) returns the InferenceContext for the node

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: ctx is non-null for a valid node with a context, so we skip the error return

step_by_step_execution.description: ctx->set_output(output_port, shape) writes shape into outputs_[output_port] without any bounds check on output_port

in_state.value: valid InferenceContext with outputs_ array of size num_outputs

out_state.value: shape written at out-of-bounds index, corrupting heap memory

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches the CVE: output_port is not validated before being used as an index in set_output, leading to a heap OOB write. The fix adds bounds checking for output_port, which is precisely what the finding identifies.

actual_issue_summary: In SetUnknownShape, the output_port parameter was not validated before being passed to ctx->set_output(), allowing an attacker to supply an out-of-bounds index (negative or >= num_outputs) causing a heap out-of-bounds write. The fix adds a bounds check ensuring output_port is in [0, num_outputs).

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. A malicious user can cause a use after free behavior when decoding PNG images. After `png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode)` gets called, the values of `decode.width` and `decode.height` are in an unspecified state. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: When op_type_ is 'DecodeGif', the output tensor is allocated with 4 dimensions {1, height, width, decode.channels}, but in the DT_FLOAT code path, it is accessed via output->tensor<float, 3>() which expects exactly 3 dimensions. TensorFlow's tensor<T,N>() performs a CHECK that dims()==N, causing a process abort (crash) when this check fails.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via process crash. An attacker who can trigger DecodeGif with a PNG image and DT_FLOAT dtype causes the serving process to abort.

step_by_step_execution.description: Output tensor allocated with 4 dimensions for DecodeGif
DT_FLOAT path accesses output as 3-D tensor

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential dimension mismatch issue unrelated to CVE-2022-23584. The actual CVE is a use-after-free: png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode) is called before OP_REQUIRES which then reads decode.width and decode.height in the error message, accessing freed memory. The fix removes the CommonFreeDecode call before the OP_REQUIRES. The finding about DecodeGif + DT_FLOAT dimension mismatch is a completely different issue.

actual_issue_summary: Use-after-free in DecodePng: png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode) frees the decode context, then the subsequent OP_REQUIRES macro accesses decode.width and decode.height in the error message string, reading freed memory. The fix removes the CommonFreeDecode call before the OP_REQUIRES, allowing the normal cleanup path to handle freeing.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `GetInitOp` is vulnerable to a crash caused by dereferencing a null pointer. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The result of .find(kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey) on the outputs() map is dereferenced without checking if it equals .end(). If the signature_def contains the key kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey at the top level, but the corresponding SignatureDef's outputs map does NOT contain that same key, the find returns end(), and dereferencing it (->second.name()) is undefined behavior (out-of-bounds read / crash).
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential information disclosure from reading invalid memory. An attacker who controls the SavedModel (e.g., a malicious model file) can craft a MetaGraphDef where the signature_def map has the init op key but the inner outputs map does not, triggering undefined behavior.

state.value: end() (key not found in outputs map)

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled map that contains key kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey
attacker-controlled map that does NOT contain key kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey

step_by_step_execution.description: Find kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey in signature_def map

out_state.value: valid iterator pointing to entry with kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The key exists in sig_def_map, so the iterator is valid and not end()

step_by_step_execution.description: Call .find(kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey) on the outputs() map of the found signature def. Since outputs map does not contain this key, the result is end().

in_state.value: map that does NOT contain kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey

step_by_step_execution.description: Dereference the end() iterator via ->second.name(), causing null pointer dereference / undefined behavior

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-23577: the inner find() result on the outputs map is not checked against end() before dereferencing, leading to a null pointer dereference / crash. The fix adds exactly this check.

actual_issue_summary: In GetInitOp, after finding kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey in the signature_def map, the code searches for the same key in the inner outputs map but dereferences the result without checking if the key was found. If the inner map doesn't contain the key, this dereferences end(), causing a null pointer dereference crash (CVE-2022-23577). The fix adds a check for the inner find result against end() before dereferencing.

cve_desc: uWebSockets 18.11.0 and 18.12.0 has a stack-based buffer overflow in uWS::TopicTree::trimTree (called from uWS::TopicTree::unsubscribeAll). NOTE: the vendor's position is that this is "a minor issue or not even an issue at all" because the developer of an application (that uses uWebSockets) should not be allowing the large number of triggered topics to accumulate

undesired_operation.description: triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++] writes beyond the bounds of the triggeredTopics array (size 64). The drain check only occurs at function entry, but within a single invocation of publish(), multiple entries can be added to triggeredTopics in the loop (one per segment with a terminatingWildcardChild, plus the final exact match after the loop), causing numTriggeredTopics to exceed 63 and overflow the fixed-size array.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds write on the triggeredTopics array. An attacker who can publish a topic with many segments (e.g., 'a/b/c/d/.../z') to a topic tree where each intermediate node has a unique untriggered terminatingWildcardChild can corrupt memory past the triggeredTopics buffer, potentially leading to code execution or a crash.

initial_state.value: 0 to 64 (any value up to 64 at function entry)

step_by_step_execution.description: At function entry, if numTriggeredTopics == 64, drain() is called resetting it to 0. But drain only runs once at the top.
Loop iterates over topic segments. For each segment, if iterator->terminatingWildcardChild exists and is not yet triggered, triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++] is executed without a bounds check.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: With enough topic segments each having an untriggered terminatingWildcardChild, numTriggeredTopics can reach and exceed 64 within the loop body, since no drain check is performed before each write.

step_by_step_execution.description: After the loop, triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++] = iterator writes past the end of the 64-element array.

judgments.reasoning: The finding precisely identifies the CVE-2020-36406 vulnerability: the drain() check at function entry is insufficient because multiple triggered topics can accumulate within a single publish() call, overflowing the fixed-size triggeredTopics[64] array. The fix moves the drain check to immediately before each write to triggeredTopics, which is exactly what the commit does.

actual_issue_summary: The drain() call that prevents triggeredTopics array overflow was only at the start of publish(), but the function can add multiple entries to the array during a single call (in the loop for terminatingWildcardChild matches and after the loop for the exact match). This allows numTriggeredTopics to exceed the array size of 64, causing a stack-based buffer overflow. The fix moves the drain check to immediately before each triggeredTopics write.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions several TensorFlow operations are missing validation for the shapes of the tensor arguments involved in the call. Depending on the API, this can result in undefined behavior and segfault or `CHECK`-fail related crashes but in some scenarios writes and reads from heap populated arrays are also possible. We have discovered these issues internally via tooling while working on improving/testing GPU op determinism. As such, we don't have reproducers and there will be multiple fixes for these issues. These fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: Division by zero when computing num_groups if filter.dim_size(2) (patch_depth) is 0. An attacker who controls the filter tensor shape can trigger a crash.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (floating point exception / SIGFPE)

step_by_step_execution.description: patch_depth is read from filter.dim_size(2)
num_groups = in_depth / patch_depth triggers division by zero

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE fix addresses a segfault on OOM (out-of-memory) in Conv2D by switching from direct Tensor constructor allocation to ctx->allocate_temp() with OP_REQUIRES_OK error checking. The finding describes a division-by-zero issue which is unrelated to the OOM handling fix.

undesired_operation.description: Division by zero when computing tensor.dim_size(3) / num_groups inside pre_shuffle and post_shuffle lambdas, if num_groups is 0. This happens when in_depth (input.dim_size(3)) is 0 while patch_depth is non-zero.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (SIGFPE)

step_by_step_execution.description: in_depth is read as 0 from input.dim_size(3)
patch_depth is read as non-zero from filter.dim_size(2)
num_groups = 0 / patch_depth = 0
Division by num_groups (0) in pre_shuffle/post_shuffle lambdas

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE fix addresses a segfault on OOM by replacing direct Tensor constructor allocation with ctx->allocate_temp() wrapped in OP_REQUIRES_OK for proper error handling. This finding describes a division-by-zero issue which is unrelated to the OOM fix.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a segfault caused by out-of-memory (OOM) conditions during Conv2D grouped convolution. Temporary tensors (input_shuffled, filter_shuffled, output_shuffled) were allocated using the Tensor constructor directly, which does not propagate allocation failures gracefully. The fix switches to ctx->allocate_temp() with OP_REQUIRES_OK, which properly handles OOM by returning an error status instead of crashing.

cve_desc: Glewlwyd 2.0.0, fixed in 2.6.1 is affected by an incorrect access control vulnerability. One user can attempt to log in as another user without its password.

undesired_operation.description: When password authentication fails, if the user has an existing session, user_session_update is called with the attacker-supplied username and flag 1 (same as successful auth), associating the unauthenticated username with the existing session.
undesired_operation.impact: Authentication bypass — an attacker with any existing valid session can associate an arbitrary username with their session by sending a login request with that username and an incorrect password.

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled JSON body with username and wrong password

step_by_step_execution.description: j_param is parsed from request body containing a username and incorrect password

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Valid JSON was provided
Username is provided and non-empty
No scheme_type provided, so password auth path is taken
Password is provided and non-empty

step_by_step_execution.description: auth_check_user_credentials is called and returns G_ERROR_UNAUTHORIZED because password is wrong

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Authentication failed, result is G_ERROR_UNAUTHORIZED not G_OK

step_by_step_execution.description: get_session_id retrieves existing session_uid from request cookie (attacker has a valid session)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Attacker already has a valid session cookie, so session_uid is non-NULL

step_by_step_execution.description: user_session_update is called with attacker-supplied victim username and flag 1, updating the session to be associated with the victim user despite failed authentication

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely identifies the CVE-2021-45379 vulnerability. The diff removes exactly the code that calls user_session_update on the failed authentication path, which allowed an attacker with an existing session to associate another user's username with their session by providing that username with an incorrect password.

actual_issue_summary: When password authentication fails (G_ERROR_UNAUTHORIZED), the code incorrectly calls user_session_update with the attacker-supplied username and the existing session ID, potentially allowing a user to associate another user's username with their session without providing the correct password. The fix removes this user_session_update call from the failed authentication path.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `AddManySparseToTensorsMap` is vulnerable to an integer overflow which results in a `CHECK`-fail when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, an assert failure based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: new_num_elements is declared as 'int' (32-bit) but receives the return value of MultiplyWithoutOverflow which operates on int64_t values. When the int64_t result of the multiplication is larger than INT_MAX but the truncated 32-bit value is non-negative, the overflow check (new_num_elements < 0) is bypassed. This allows construction of a TensorShape with a dangerously large number of elements, potentially leading to heap buffer overflow or other memory corruption downstream.
undesired_operation.impact: An attacker can craft input sparse_shape values such that the product of dimensions overflows int64_t (detected by MultiplyWithoutOverflow returning a negative int64_t) but after truncation to 32-bit int becomes non-negative, bypassing the overflow check. This allows creating tensors with unexpected shapes, potentially leading to denial of service or memory corruption.

state.value: truncated 32-bit int from int64_t result of MultiplyWithoutOverflow, possibly non-negative despite overflow

initial_state.value: user-provided int64_t vector of sparse tensor dimensions

step_by_step_execution.description: new_num_elements is initialized as int (32-bit) with value 1
MultiplyWithoutOverflow returns int64_t result, which is truncated to 32-bit int when assigned to new_num_elements

out_state.value: truncated 32-bit value, possibly non-negative despite actual overflow

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Because the int64_t result was truncated to 32-bit int, the resulting value may be non-negative even when the true product overflows, so the overflow is not detected
overflow_ocurred remains false because the truncation masked the overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: TensorShape is constructed directly from user-provided dimensions without proper overflow protection

out_state.value: TensorShape constructed with overflowing dimensions, triggering CHECK-fail

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue of CVE-2022-23568: the faulty overflow check using a 32-bit int variable to hold int64_t results from MultiplyWithoutOverflow, which allows the overflow to go undetected. The fix replaces this manual check with TensorShape::BuildTensorShape which properly handles overflow. The finding accurately describes the integer overflow vulnerability and its mechanism.

actual_issue_summary: The overflow check in AddManySparseToTensorsMap used a 32-bit int (new_num_elements) to store results from MultiplyWithoutOverflow (which returns int64_t), making the check ineffective. Even when the check passed, the TensorShape constructor was called directly with user-provided dimensions without safe overflow handling, leading to a CHECK-fail (denial of service). The fix replaces the faulty manual overflow check and direct TensorShape construction with TensorShape::BuildTensorShape, which properly validates dimensions and returns an error status instead of crashing.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library. In version 2.11.1 and prior, if incoming RTCP XR message contain block, the data field is not checked against the received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP XR. A malicious actor can send a RTCP XR message with an invalid packet size.

undesired_operation.description: The while loop does not validate that each report block's full size (rb_len+1 32-bit words) fits within the remaining packet data before setting pointers like rb_stats or rb_voip_mtc. After the loop, the code dereferences deep fields of these structures (e.g., rb_stats->jitter_min, rb_voip_mtc->jb_abs_max), reading memory beyond the actual packet buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read of heap/stack memory beyond the packet buffer. An attacker sending a crafted RTCP XR packet can leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash (denial of service).

state.value: Points within packet buffer but block data extends beyond packet size
Set to rb_hdr without verifying the full struct fits in the packet

Actual received packet size in bytes

out_state.value: Value from packet header, e.g. a value that passes the size check but allows blocks to claim large sizes

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The attacker crafts the packet so the overall length field passes this check, but individual block lengths inside can still exceed what fits in the actual packet.
rb_hdr starts at rtcp_xr->buf which is within the packet, so the while loop is entered.

relevant_state.value: Points to first report block header within packet

out_state.value: A value that makes rb_hdr appear valid per the while loop condition, but the actual struct extends beyond the packet buffer

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Attacker sets rb_len to a nonzero value so the switch block executes.

relevant_state.value: nonzero, e.g. matching the expected block length

step_by_step_execution.description: In the switch case, rb_stats or rb_voip_mtc is set to rb_hdr without checking that sizeof(*rb_stats) or sizeof(*rb_voip_mtc) bytes fit within [rb_hdr, pkt+size).

out_state.value: Points to rb_hdr, but the full struct extends past pkt+size
Points to rb_hdr, but the full struct extends past pkt+size

step_by_step_execution.description: After the loop, code reads deep fields of rb_stats (e.g., rb_stats->jitter_min) or rb_voip_mtc (e.g., rb_voip_mtc->jb_abs_max), which are beyond the packet buffer boundary, causing out-of-bounds read.

in_state.value: Points into packet buffer but struct extends beyond pkt+size

out_state.value: Read from memory beyond packet buffer (OOB read)

judgments.reasoning: The finding accurately describes the CVE-2021-43845 vulnerability: the RTCP XR block data pointers are set without validating that the full structure fits within the received packet size, leading to out-of-bounds reads. The fix adds exactly these bounds checks before setting the pointers. CWE-125 matches.

actual_issue_summary: In pjmedia_rtcp_xr_rx_rtcp_xr(), when parsing RTCP XR report blocks, the code sets pointers (rb_rr_time, rb_dlrr, rb_stats, rb_voip_mtc) to report block headers without verifying that the full structure size fits within the received packet buffer. This allows an attacker to send a crafted RTCP XR packet with a valid overall length but truncated block data, causing out-of-bounds reads when the fields of these structures are later accessed. The fix adds bounds checks ensuring each block's full struct size fits within [rb_hdr, pkt+size] before setting the pointer.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementations of `Sparse*Cwise*` ops are vulnerable to integer overflows. These can be used to trigger large allocations (so, OOM based denial of service) or `CHECK`-fails when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, assert failures based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: TensorShape is constructed from user-provided sp_shape values without validating they are non-negative. Negative dimension values in sp_shape cause a CHECK failure (assertion crash) in TensorShape's constructor, leading to denial of service.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via assertion failure crash. An attacker can provide a crafted sp_shape tensor with negative dimension values to crash the TensorFlow process.

state.value: user-provided values that may contain negative or very large integers

initial_state.value: user-provided int64 vector from sp_shape input tensor, may contain arbitrary values including negative numbers or very large values

step_by_step_execution.description: shape_vec is read from the user-provided sp_shape tensor

out_state.value: vector of int64 values from sp_shape, potentially containing negative or overflow-inducing values

step_by_step_execution.description: TensorShape is constructed directly from shape_vec without validation, triggering CHECK failure on invalid dimensions

in_state.value: contains negative or very large dimension values

out_state.value: CHECK failure / crash before object is fully constructed

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core vulnerability: TensorShape is constructed directly from user-provided sp_shape values using TensorShape(shape_vec), which can trigger CHECK failures or integer overflows. The fix replaces this with TensorShape::BuildTensorShape which returns a Status instead of crashing. While the finding focuses on negative values and CWE-617 (assertion failure) rather than CWE-190 (integer overflow), both are consequences of the same root cause identified in CVE-2022-23567, which explicitly mentions CHECK-fails when building TensorShape objects.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerable code constructs a TensorShape directly from user-provided sp_shape dimensions using `TensorShape(shape_vec)`, which can trigger integer overflows (for large values) or CHECK-failures (for negative values). The fix uses `TensorShape::BuildTensorShape()` which validates the dimensions and returns a status error instead of crashing.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. Under certain scenarios, Grappler component of TensorFlow can trigger a null pointer dereference. There are 2 places where this can occur, for the same malicious alteration of a `SavedModel` file (fixing the first one would trigger the same dereference in the second place). First, during constant folding, the `GraphDef` might not have the required nodes for the binary operation. If a node is missing, the correposning `mul_*child` would be null, and the dereference in the subsequent line would be incorrect. We have a similar issue during `IsIdentityConsumingSwitch`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: graph.GetNode() may return nullptr when the node name from tensor_id is not found in the graph, and the result is dereferenced without a null check via IsSwitch(*input_node)
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (null pointer dereference). If an attacker can craft a graph where an Identity node's input references a node name not present in the MutableGraphView, dereferencing the null pointer causes a segfault.

out_state.value: parsed TensorId with node() pointing to a non-existent node name

in_state.value: name of a node not in the graph

step_by_step_execution.description: input_node is dereferenced via IsSwitch(*input_node), causing null pointer dereference

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly identifies the null pointer dereference in IsIdentityConsumingSwitch that is one of the two places described in CVE-2022-23589, matching the diff fix precisely.

actual_issue_summary: In TensorFlow's Grappler component, the IsIdentityConsumingSwitch function dereferences the result of graph.GetNode() without checking for nullptr. If a SavedModel is maliciously altered so that an Identity node references a non-existent input node, GetNode returns nullptr and the subsequent IsSwitch(*input_node) causes a null pointer dereference crash. The fix adds a nullptr check before the dereference.

cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository gpac/gpac prior to 1.1.0.

The post-loop check 'if (ptr->entries) gf_free(ptr->entries)' confirms the developer expected entries could be NULL, but this guard is missing before the loop.

actual_issue_summary: In gitn_box_del, ptr->entries could be NULL while ptr->nb_entries > 0, leading to a NULL pointer dereference when iterating over entries in the for loop. The fix adds a NULL check on ptr->entries before the loop.

cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in Homebrew mruby prior to 3.2.

undesired_operation.description: pop_n(len*2) is called in the NODE_KW_REST_ARGS branch when val is 0 (false). Since codegen with val=0 does not push values onto the virtual stack, but len is still incremented in the else branch, pop_n underflows the stack pointer (cursp), causing out-of-bounds memory access or corrupted bytecode generation.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack pointer underflow leads to corrupted bytecode generation, which could cause out-of-bounds memory access, denial of service via crash, or potentially arbitrary code execution when the generated bytecode is executed.

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop iterates on a non-KW_REST_ARGS node, calling codegen with val=0 (no push) and incrementing len

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Next node in the tree is a NODE_KW_REST_ARGS node
len is 1 from the previous iteration, and val is not checked

step_by_step_execution.description: pop_n(len*2) is called with len=1, popping 2 entries from the virtual stack. Since val=0, codegen never pushed any values, so this underflows the stack pointer.

in_state.value: some base value with no extra pushes

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the root cause of CVE-2022-0481: when val is 0, pop_n and pop operations are performed without corresponding pushes, causing stack pointer underflow. The fix adds `val &&` checks to guard these operations. While the CVE is classified as CWE-476 (NULL Pointer Dereference) — likely the crash manifestation — the finding correctly identifies the underlying bug that the commit fixes. The stack underflow likely manifests as a NULL pointer dereference.

actual_issue_summary: When gen_hash is called with val=0, the code in the NODE_KW_REST_ARGS branch performed pop_n/pop operations that assumed values had been pushed onto the virtual stack. Since val=0 means codegen doesn't push values, this caused stack pointer underflow, manifesting as a NULL pointer dereference. The fix adds `val &&` guards before the pop operations.

cve_desc: A Vulnerability of LG Electronic web OS TV Emulator could allow an attacker to escalate privileges and overwrite certain files. This vulnerability is due to wrong environment setting. An attacker could exploit this vulnerability through crafted configuration files and executable files.

undesired_operation.description: When argc == 9 and argv[8] equals "*", arg_start is set to 9, and then argv[arg_start][0] (i.e., argv[9][0]) is accessed, which is an out-of-bounds read past the end of the argv array (valid indices are 0..8).
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read from a crafted IRC WHO reply. Could cause a crash (denial of service) or information disclosure depending on what lies beyond the argv array in memory.

argc is 9, so 9 > 8 is true; we enter the block.

argv[arg_start][0] is accessed, i.e. argv[9][0], but argv only has indices 0..8. This is an out-of-bounds read.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact bug fixed by the commit. When argc == 9 and argv[8] == "*", arg_start is set to 9, causing an out-of-bounds access on argv[9]. The fix adds a check (argc > 9) before setting arg_start to 9. While the CVE metadata appears mismatched (CVE-2020-9759 describes an LG webOS TV issue), the commit message and diff clearly show this is a crash fix for malformed IRC 352 messages, and the finding accurately describes that vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: When a malformed IRC 352 (WHO) message has exactly 9 arguments with argv[8]=="*", arg_start is set to 9 without checking that argv[9] exists, causing an out-of-bounds array access and crash. The fix adds an (argc > 9) check before allowing arg_start to be set to 9.

cve_desc: Depending on the way the format strings in the card label are crafted it's possible to leak kernel stack memory. There is also the possibility for DoS due to the v4l2loopback kernel module crashing when providing the card label on request (reproduce e.g. with many %s modifiers in a row).

undesired_operation.description: dev->card_label is passed as the format string argument to snprintf. If card_label contains format specifiers (e.g. %s, %n, %x), snprintf will interpret them, leading to information disclosure from the stack or potentially memory corruption via %n.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure of stack contents via format specifiers like %x, or potential memory corruption/crash via %n if card_label is attacker-controlled (e.g. set via ioctl).

initial_state.value: user-controlled card label string, potentially containing format specifiers

snprintf is called with dev->card_label as the format string directly, without an explicit %s format specifier

out_state.value: contains formatted output interpreting card_label as format string, reading stack values

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly identifies the CVE-2022-2652 format string vulnerability: dev->card_label is used as the format string in snprintf without a proper %s format specifier, allowing format string attacks. The fix changes the call to snprintf(cap->card, labellen, "%s", dev->card_label).

actual_issue_summary: In vidioc_querycap, dev->card_label (user-controllable) is passed directly as the format string to snprintf() instead of as a string argument with a "%s" format specifier. This allows format string attacks that can leak kernel stack memory or cause a denial of service crash.

cve_desc: In Deark before v1.5.8, a specially crafted input file can cause a division by zero in (src/fmtutil.c) because of the value of pixelsize.

actual_issue_summary: In fmtutil_macbitmap_read_pixmap_only_fields, bi->pixelsize is read from user-controlled input and used as a divisor without checking for zero, causing a division-by-zero crash. The fix adds a guard `if(bi->pixelsize>0)` before the division.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, multiple TensorFlow operations misbehave in eager mode when the resource handle provided to them is invalid. In graph mode, it would have been impossible to perform these API calls, but migration to TF 2.x eager mode opened up this vulnerability. If the resource handle is empty, then a reference is bound to a null pointer inside TensorFlow codebase (various codepaths). This is undefined behavior. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

undesired_operation.description: absl::get<Device*>(op.Device()) will throw absl::bad_variant_access if op.Device() holds a string variant instead of a Device*. This causes an unhandled exception and process crash.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash — an attacker who can submit an EagerOperation whose device is specified as a string name (rather than a Device pointer) triggers an uncaught exception when the else branch is reached for a non-function op with a non-null, non-CPU tensor device.

initial_state.value: could hold either a Device* or a string

dtype is not DT_RESOURCE so we go to else
The dtype does not use host memory
op is not a function and device is non-null and not CPU

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-29207) is about empty resource handles causing null pointer dereferences when tensor->NumElements() == 0 and tensor->flat<ResourceHandle>()(0) is accessed. The fix adds a check for empty tensors before that access. This finding describes a completely different hypothetical issue with absl::get<Device*> in the else branch, which is unrelated to the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: When a DT_RESOURCE tensor handle has zero elements, the code accesses tensor->flat<ResourceHandle>()(0) without checking, leading to undefined behavior (null pointer dereference). The fix adds a check for tensor->NumElements() == 0 and returns an InvalidArgument error before the access.

cve_desc: Buffer overflow in the send_control_msg function in hw/char/virtio-serial-bus.c in QEMU before 2.4.0 allows guest users to cause a denial of service (QEMU process crash) via a crafted virtio control message.

undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow — a malicious guest can supply a small virtqueue buffer, causing the host to write past the end of the guest-provided memory mapping, potentially leading to host code execution or denial of service (VM escape).

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the CVE-2015-5745 vulnerability: memcpy writes 'len' bytes into a guest-provided buffer without checking that the buffer is large enough, causing a buffer overflow. The fix replaces the unchecked memcpy with iov_from_buf which properly handles scatter-gather lists and their sizes.

actual_issue_summary: In send_control_msg, memcpy(elem.in_sg[0].iov_base, buf, len) writes 'len' bytes into the first scatter-gather buffer without verifying that the buffer is large enough or that the data is properly distributed across all scatter-gather entries. A guest can provide a small buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. The fix replaces memcpy with iov_from_buf which safely copies data across the full iovec array respecting individual buffer sizes.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can cause a denial of service in `boosted_trees_create_quantile_stream_resource` by using negative arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantile_ops.cc#L96) does not validate that `num_streams` only contains non-negative numbers. In turn, [this results in using this value to allocate memory](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantiles/quantile_stream_resource.h#L31-L40). However, `reserve` receives an unsigned integer so there is an implicit conversion from a negative value to a large positive unsigned. This results in a crash from the standard library. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 8a84f7a2b5a2b27ecf88d25bad9ac777cd2f7992. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: num_streams is read from user input as int64 but never validated. A negative or zero value for num_streams is passed directly to the QuantileStreamResource constructor, which could cause undefined behavior, integer overflow in internal allocations, or denial of service.
code_snippets: auto result = new QuantileStreamResource(epsilon, max_elements_, num_streams);
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service or potentially worse — attacker provides a negative or zero num_streams value that is used unchecked in resource allocation/initialization.

step_by_step_execution.description: num_streams_t is read from the input tensor named kNumStreamsName
num_streams is extracted as int64 scalar from the input tensor

step_by_step_execution.description: num_streams is passed directly to QuantileStreamResource constructor, where it is implicitly converted to a large unsigned value when used in reserve(), causing a crash

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the same vulnerability as CVE-2021-37661: missing validation of num_streams allowing negative values, which leads to denial of service via implicit conversion to a large unsigned value in memory allocation. The CWE-20 (Improper Input Validation) is consistent with the root cause described in the CVE (CWE-703).

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerable function reads num_streams from user input but does not validate that it is non-negative. When a negative value is provided, it gets implicitly converted to a very large unsigned integer during memory allocation (reserve), causing a crash and denial of service. The fix adds a check ensuring num_streams >= 0.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseCountSparseOutput` is vulnerable to a heap overflow. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The variable 'batch' is declared as 'int' but assigned from 'indices_values(idx, 0)' which is int64_t. An attacker can provide an index value like 2147483648 (0x80000000) that truncates to a negative int. The bounds check 'batch >= num_batches' fails to catch negative batch values (since negative < positive), so per_batch_counts[batch] is accessed with a negative index, which converts to a huge size_t, causing out-of-bounds memory access on the vector.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds memory write leading to potential arbitrary code execution or denial of service via crash. An attacker controls the indices tensor and can craft values that truncate to negative integers.

In the loop, batch is assigned from indices_values(idx, 0) with int64->int truncation. Attacker sets indices_values(0,0) = 2147483648.

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a heap overflow path in SparseCountSparseOutput through insufficient validation of indices, which is the core issue of CVE-2022-21740. The int64->int truncation allows bypassing the batch bounds check, leading to out-of-bounds write.

undesired_operation.description: The variable 'num_batches' is declared as 'int' but assigned from 'shape_vector(0)' which is int64_t. An attacker can provide a dense_shape with first dimension e.g. 3000000000 (> INT_MAX) which passes the >= 0 check but truncates to a negative int. This negative value is passed to BatchedMap<W>(num_batches) constructor (likely std::vector), where the negative int is converted to a huge size_t, causing excessive memory allocation or crash.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or excessive memory allocation. The attacker controls the shape tensor.

BatchedMap<W>(num_batches) is called with negative num_batches

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes another path to trigger the heap overflow / memory corruption in SparseCountSparseOutput through insufficient validation of the shape input. The fix addresses this by restructuring input handling and adding proper validation. This is a component of the same CVE-2022-21740 vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2022-21740 is a heap overflow in TensorFlow's SparseCountSparseOutput due to insufficient input validation. The vulnerable code lacks proper checks on tensor shapes, dimensions consistency, and has integer truncation issues (int64_t to int) for batch indices and num_batches. The fix adds early validation of input tensor shapes and dimensional consistency, preventing crafted inputs from causing out-of-bounds memory access.

cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_value_own_enumerate at src/njs_value.c.

undesired_operation.description: In the fast array path, array->start[n] is read using 'delete' computed from a stale 'length' value. If njs_value_to_integer (for args[1] or args[2]) triggers a valueOf callback that shrinks the array, 'delete' can exceed the current array->length, causing out-of-bounds read from array->start.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read of heap memory, potentially leaking sensitive data. Additionally, array->length += delta can underflow, corrupting the array length and enabling further exploitation.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE is about uninitialized entries in the 'deleted' fast array when 'this' is a slow object with gaps. The fix adds an else branch to call njs_set_invalid() for missing properties. This finding describes a hypothetical valueOf callback causing stale length, which is unrelated to the actual fix.

undesired_operation.description: In the fast array path for the main splice operation, array->length += delta causes array->length to underflow. With array->length = 0 (shrunk by valueOf) and delta = -100, array->length wraps to a very large value if unsigned, corrupting the array's length metadata and enabling subsequent arbitrary OOB access.
code_snippets:
ret = njs_array_copy_within(vm, this, start + items, start + delete,
                                        array->length - (start + delete), 0);
undesired_operation.impact: Integer underflow of array->length corrupts the array metadata. If array->length is unsigned, it wraps to a very large value, making the array appear to have billions of elements. This enables subsequent out-of-bounds reads and writes on the array from JavaScript code, leading to arbitrary code execution.

judgments.reasoning: Same as above - the actual vulnerability is about uninitialized fast array entries in the 'deleted' array when iterating over a slow 'this' object with gaps. This finding about integer underflow via valueOf callbacks is a different hypothetical issue unrelated to the CVE fix.

actual_issue_summary: When Array.prototype.splice() is called with a slow (non-fast-array) 'this' argument that has gaps (missing properties), and the 'deleted' result array is a fast array, the entries in deleted->start[] corresponding to the gaps are left uninitialized. The fix adds an else branch that calls njs_set_invalid() on those entries, preventing later use of uninitialized memory (which caused a segfault in njs_value_own_enumerate).

cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the btrfs_rm_device function in fs/btrfs/volumes.c in the Linux Kernel, where triggering the bug requires ‘CAP_SYS_ADMIN’. This flaw allows a local attacker to crash the system or leak kernel internal information. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via kernel crash (NULL pointer dereference in kernel context)

invalid device id (e.g., 3, not present in filesystem)

PTR_ERR(device) is -ENOENT so the first part is true; then strcmp is called on NULL device_path, triggering NULL pointer dereference before the condition can complete

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-3739: a NULL pointer dereference when strcmp is called on a NULL device_path in btrfs_rm_device. The fix adds a `device_path &&` check before the strcmp call.

actual_issue_summary: In btrfs_rm_device(), when a device is looked up by devid only (device_path is NULL) and the device is not found (-ENOENT), strcmp(device_path, "missing") is called on a NULL pointer, causing a kernel NULL pointer dereference. The fix adds a NULL check for device_path before calling strcmp.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of division in TFLite is [vulnerable to a division by 0 error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/div.cc). There is no check that the divisor tensor does not contain zero elements. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 1e206baedf8bef0334cca3eb92bab134ef525a28. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The return value of EvalDiv<kernel_type>() is not checked. If EvalDiv encounters an error (e.g., division by zero, internal failure), the error is silently ignored and the function returns kTfLiteOk, causing the caller to treat potentially uninitialized or corrupt output tensor data as valid.
code_snippets: EvalDiv<kernel_type>(context, node, params, data, input1, input2, output);
undesired_operation.impact: If EvalDiv fails internally (e.g., due to division by zero or other error conditions), the caller receives kTfLiteOk and proceeds to use the output tensor which may contain uninitialized, stale, or corrupt data. This can lead to incorrect computation results being silently accepted, potential information disclosure of heap/stack contents through uninitialized output memory, or downstream crashes.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2021-37683 / CWE-369) is about a missing check that the divisor tensor (input2) does not contain zero elements, leading to division by zero. The fix adds explicit pre-validation of input2 data for zero values before calling EvalDiv. The finding instead claims the issue is an unchecked return value from EvalDiv (CWE-252), which is a different problem. The fix does not add return value checking — it adds zero-element validation on the divisor tensor. The finding misidentifies the root cause and the vulnerability mechanism.

actual_issue_summary: The TFLite division operator (div.cc) did not check whether the divisor tensor (input2) contained zero elements before performing division. For INT32 and UINT8 types, this leads to a division-by-zero error. The fix adds a macro TF_LITE_CHECK_DIV_NON_ZERO that iterates over input2 elements and ensures none are zero before proceeding with the division operation.

cve_desc: In eager mode, TensorFlow before versions 1.15.4, 2.0.3, 2.1.2, 2.2.1 and 2.3.1 does not set the session state. Hence, calling `tf.raw_ops.GetSessionHandle` or `tf.raw_ops.GetSessionHandleV2` results in a null pointer dereference In linked snippet, in eager mode, `ctx->session_state()` returns `nullptr`. Since code immediately dereferences this, we get a segmentation fault. The issue is patched in commit 9a133d73ae4b4664d22bd1aa6d654fec13c52ee1, and is released in TensorFlow versions 1.15.4, 2.0.3, 2.1.2, 2.2.1, or 2.3.1.

undesired_operation.description: ctx->session_state() may return nullptr, and the result is immediately dereferenced by calling GetNewId() without a null check, leading to a null pointer dereference.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash. An attacker who can trigger this op in a context where session_state is null (e.g., eager execution mode) can crash the TensorFlow process.

state.value: nullptr (in eager mode, session state is not set)

GetNewId() is immediately called on the nullptr return value, causing a null pointer dereference

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2020-15204: in eager mode, ctx->session_state() returns nullptr, and the code dereferences it without a null check, causing a crash. The fix adds the null check before dereferencing.

actual_issue_summary: In eager mode, ctx->session_state() returns nullptr. The code in GetSessionHandle/GetSessionHandleV2 immediately dereferences this by calling ->GetNewId(), causing a null pointer dereference and crash. The fix adds a null check with OP_REQUIRES before dereferencing.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.StagePeek` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `index` is a scalar but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

undesired_operation.description: A negative int from user input is implicitly converted to a very large std::size_t value, which is then passed to buf->Peek(index, &tuple), causing an out-of-bounds read/access on the buffer's internal data structure.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential out-of-bounds memory read. An attacker providing a negative integer input causes an enormous unsigned index to be passed to Peek.

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled tensor with negative integer value

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-29195) is about the code assuming `index` is a scalar tensor without validation. When a non-scalar tensor is passed as `index`, calling `.scalar<int>()()` on it triggers a CHECK-failure (assertion crash), resulting in denial of service. The finding instead describes a signed-to-unsigned integer conversion issue with negative values, which is a different vulnerability mechanism. While both involve the same line of code, the CVE is specifically about missing shape validation (non-scalar input), not about the sign conversion of the scalar value.

actual_issue_summary: The StagePeek implementation calls `.scalar<int>()()` on the `index` input without first validating that the input tensor is actually a scalar. Passing a non-scalar tensor triggers an internal CHECK-failure assertion, causing a denial of service crash. The fix removes the index-based Peek entirely, replacing it with a Get call.

cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

undesired_operation.description: The while loop condition evaluates VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + s - 1)) before checking s > 0 due to C's left-to-right short-circuit evaluation of &&. When s reaches 0, this reads *(bd->textstart - 1), an out-of-bounds read. Furthermore, pnew is decremented past the start of the allocated buffer, and *pnew = NUL writes before the heap buffer (heap buffer underwrite).
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read of memory before bd->textstart, and heap buffer underwrite via *pnew = NUL when pnew has been decremented below the allocated buffer start. Could lead to information disclosure, crash, or potentially arbitrary code execution.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the root cause: the while loop checks VIM_ISWHITE before checking s > 0, allowing an out-of-bounds read when s equals 0. It also correctly identifies the consequence: pnew can be decremented below the buffer start, leading to a heap buffer underwrite via *pnew = NUL. This matches CVE-2022-0407 (heap-based buffer overflow / reading before the start of the line) and the fix which swaps the condition order to check s > 0 first.

actual_issue_summary: In yank_copy_line(), the while loop condition checks VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + s - 1)) before checking s > 0. When s is 0, this reads memory before bd->textstart (reading before the start of the line). The fix swaps the condition order so s > 0 is checked first, preventing the out-of-bounds access via short-circuit evaluation.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. A buffer overflow vulnerability in versions 2.12 and prior affects applications that use PJSIP DNS resolution. It doesn't affect PJSIP users who utilize an external resolver. This vulnerability is related to CVE-2023-27585. The difference is that this issue is in parsing the query record `parse_rr()`, while the issue in CVE-2023-27585 is in `parse_query()`. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. A workaround is to disable DNS resolution in PJSIP config (by setting `nameserver_count` to zero) or use an external resolver instead.

undesired_operation.description: After parsing the DNS name, the code reads 4 bytes (2 for type, 2 for class) from pointer p without checking that p+4 <= max. If the packet data ends immediately after or shortly after the name, this causes an out-of-bounds read.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read leading to information disclosure or crash (denial of service) when processing a crafted DNS packet

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches the vulnerability fixed in this commit: a missing bounds check (p + 4 > max) before reading 4 bytes of type and class fields in parse_query(). The fix adds precisely this check. While the CWE differs slightly (CWE-125 read vs CWE-787 write), the underlying issue—buffer overflow due to missing bounds check in DNS packet parsing—is the same vulnerability addressed by CVE-2022-24793.

actual_issue_summary: Buffer overflow in PJSIP DNS packet parsing in parse_query() (and related functions get_name_len/get_name). After parsing the DNS name, the code reads 4 bytes for type and class fields without checking that sufficient data remains in the packet buffer. The fix adds a bounds check 'if (p + 4 > max)' before the memcpy calls, and also fixes label length validation in get_name_len and get_name.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affeced versions during execution, `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` is supposed to set the flags in `input_has_ellipsis` vector and `*output_has_ellipsis` boolean to indicate whether there is ellipsis in the corresponding inputs and output. However, the code only changes these flags to `true` and never assigns `false`. This results in unitialized variable access if callers assume that `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` always sets these flags. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: When num_inputs is 0 (no input operands parsed from equation) and num_labels > 0 (output string has labels), the code accesses (*input_label_counts)[0][label] which is out-of-bounds because input_label_counts was resized to 0.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read leading to crash (denial of service) or potential information disclosure

step_by_step_execution.description: num_inputs is set from input_str.size(), which could be 0
input_label_counts resized to num_inputs (0)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: num_inputs is 0, not 1

step_by_step_execution.description: Short-circuit evaluation proceeds to access (*input_label_counts)[0][label]

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-41201) is specifically about uninitialized variable access: input_has_ellipsis and output_has_ellipsis flags are never set to false, only conditionally set to true. The fix adds explicit initialization to false. This finding describes a completely different issue (potential out-of-bounds access when num_inputs is 0), which is unrelated to the actual CVE.

actual_issue_summary: EinsumHelper::ParseEquation() only sets input_has_ellipsis and output_has_ellipsis to true when ellipsis is found, but never initializes them to false. This leaves the variables uninitialized when no ellipsis is present, causing undefined behavior when callers read these flags. The fix adds explicit initialization of input_has_ellipsis->at(i) = false and *output_has_ellipsis = false before the loops.

cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in tildearrow Furnace. It has been classified as problematic. This is due to an incomplete fix of CVE-2022-1211. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely but it requires user interaction. The issue got fixed with the patch 0eb02422d5161767e9983bdaa5c429762d3477ce.

undesired_operation.description: Out-of-bounds read on extFxColors array when pat->data[i][index] is >= 0xe0 with no upper bound check. The index pat->data[i][index]-0xe0 can be arbitrarily large since data values are stored as int.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure or crash — an attacker who controls song/pattern data can cause an out-of-bounds array read, potentially leaking memory contents or causing denial of service.

0x1000 >= 0xe0, so the else branch (extFxColors) is reached

judgments.reasoning: This is exactly the vulnerability the fix addresses. The fix adds a check for pat->data[i][index]>0xff before using it as an array index, preventing the OOB access on extFxColors.

undesired_operation.description: Out-of-bounds read on fxColors array when pat->data[i][index] is a negative value other than -1. The check 'pat->data[i][index]<0x10' is true for negative values, but fxColors is indexed with a negative index.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure or crash — an attacker who controls song/pattern data can cause an out-of-bounds array read with a negative index.

judgments.reasoning: This is another manifestation of the same vulnerability. The fix addresses it by casting to unsigned char (which prevents negative indexing) and adding the >0xff check. This is part of the incomplete fix for CVE-2022-1211 that CVE-2022-1289 describes.

actual_issue_summary: Pattern effect data values (pat->data[i][index]) stored as int could exceed the 0x00-0xFF range expected by the color lookup arrays (fxColors, extFxColors). Values > 0xFF caused out-of-bounds reads on extFxColors, and negative values (other than -1) caused out-of-bounds reads on fxColors. The fix adds a >0xFF bounds check and casts the value to unsigned char before using it as an array index.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2`, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/quantize_op.cc#L59) has some validation but does not check that `min_range` and `max_range` both have the same non-zero number of elements. If `axis` is provided (i.e., not `-1`), then validation should check that it is a value in range for the rank of `input` tensor and then the lengths of `min_range` and `max_range` inputs match the `axis` dimension of the `input` tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 6da6620efad397c85493b8f8667b821403516708. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.
diff:
--- vulnerable
+++ fixed
@@ -5,7 +5,50 @@
 
     int num_slices = 1;
     if (axis_ > -1) {
+      OP_REQUIRES(
+          ctx, input.dims() > axis_,
+          errors::InvalidArgument(
+              "Axis is on a zero-based index, so its value must always be less "
+              "than number of input's dims, but given axis value was ",
+              axis_, " and input's dims was ", input.dims()));
       num_slices = input.dim_size(axis_);
+      OP_REQUIRES(ctx, input_min_range.dims() == 1,
+                  errors::InvalidArgument(
+                      "If axis is specified, min_range must be a 1-D tensor "
+                      "whose size matches the axis dimension of the input and "
+                      "output tensors, but min_range dims are ",
+                      input_min_range.dims()));
+      OP_REQUIRES(ctx, input_min_range.dim_size(0) == num_slices,
+                  errors::InvalidArgument(
+                      "If axis is specified, min_range must be a 1-D tensor "
+                      "whose size matches the axis dimension of the input and "
+                      "output tensors, but min_range is a 1-D tensor of size ",
+                      input_min_range.dim_size(0),
+                      " and input's axis dimension is of size ", num_slices));
+      OP_REQUIRES(ctx, input_max_range.dims() == 1,
+                  errors::InvalidArgument(
+                      "If axis is specified, max_range must be a 1-D tensor "
+                      "whose size matches the axis dimension of the input and "
+                      "output tensors, but max_range dims are ",
+                      input_max_range.dims()));
+      OP_REQUIRES(ctx, input_max_range.dim_size(0) == num_slices,
+                  errors::InvalidArgument(
+                      "If axis is specified, max_range must be a 1-D tensor "
+                      "whose size matches the axis dimension of the input and "
+                      "output tensors, but max_range is a 1-D tensor of size ",
+                      input_max_range.dim_size(0),
+                      " and input's axis dimension is of size ", num_slices));
+    } else {
+      OP_REQUIRES(ctx, input_min_range.NumElements() == 1,
+                  errors::InvalidArgument(
+                      "If axis is not specified, min_range must contain a "
+                      "single float element, but it contains ",
+                      input_min_range.NumElements(), " elements"));
+      OP_REQUIRES(ctx, input_max_range.NumElements() == 1,
+                  errors::InvalidArgument(
+                      "If axis is not specified, max_range must contain a "
+                      "single float element, but it contains ",
+                      input_max_range.NumElements(), " elements"));
     }
 
     const TensorShape& minmax_shape = ctx->input(1).shape();

undesired_operation.description: The loop iterates num_slices times (derived from input.dim_size(axis_)), but min_ranges and max_ranges are derived from input_min_range/input_max_range tensors whose sizes are never validated against num_slices. An attacker can supply input_min_range/input_max_range tensors with fewer elements than num_slices, causing out-of-bounds read via min_ranges(i)/max_ranges(i) and out-of-bounds write via output_min_tensor->flat<float>()(i)/output_max_tensor->flat<float>()(i).
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read and write on tensor data. An attacker can read heap memory beyond the min/max range tensors and write to heap memory beyond the output min/max tensors, potentially achieving information disclosure, denial of service via crash, or code execution.

state.value: input.dim_size(axis_), potentially larger than input_min_range/input_max_range element count

initial_state.value: Attacker-controlled tensor with fewer elements than input.dim_size(axis_)
Attacker-controlled tensor with fewer elements than input.dim_size(axis_)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Attacker provides axis_ > -1 to enter the per-slice quantization path

step_by_step_execution.description: num_slices is set to input.dim_size(axis_)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: num_slices is > 1 because the input tensor's axis dimension is larger than 1

step_by_step_execution.description: min_ranges and max_ranges are created from input_min_range/input_max_range as vec<float>, with no size check against num_slices
Loop iterates i from 0 to num_slices-1, accessing min_ranges(i) and max_ranges(i) out of bounds when i >= 2

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the core issue in CVE-2021-37663: incomplete validation that min_range and max_range tensor sizes match the axis dimension of the input tensor (num_slices), leading to out-of-bounds memory access. The fix adds exactly these validation checks.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2021-37663 is caused by incomplete validation in tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2. When axis is specified, there is no check that axis is within the input tensor's rank, and no check that min_range and max_range are 1-D tensors whose sizes match the axis dimension of the input. When axis is -1, there is no check that min_range and max_range contain exactly one element. This allows null pointer dereference and out-of-bounds heap access.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When restoring tensors via raw APIs, if the tensor name is not provided, TensorFlow can be tricked into dereferencing a null pointer. Alternatively, attackers can read memory outside the bounds of heap allocated data by providing some tensor names but not enough for a successful restoration. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/47a06f40411a69c99f381495f490536972152ac0/tensorflow/core/kernels/save_restore_tensor.cc#L158-L159) retrieves the tensor list corresponding to the `tensor_name` user controlled input and immediately retrieves the tensor at the restoration index (controlled via `preferred_shard` argument). This occurs without validating that the provided list has enough values. If the list is empty this results in dereferencing a null pointer (undefined behavior). If, however, the list has some elements, if the restoration index is outside the bounds this results in heap OOB read. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e82dce6e6bd1f36a57e08fa85af213e2b2f2622. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The parameter restore_index is used to index into tensor_name_t without any bounds check, allowing an out-of-bounds read on the underlying tensor data buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read leading to information disclosure or crash. An attacker who controls restore_index or the shape of tensor_name_t (input 1) can read memory beyond the tensor buffer.

state.value: >=tensor_name_t.NumElements() (e.g., any positive value when tensor is empty, or larger than number of elements)

initial_state.value: caller-controlled integer, could be any non-negative value
user-provided tensor at input(1), could have 0 or fewer elements than restore_index

step_by_step_execution.description: tensor_name_t is retrieved from context->input(1)

out_state.value: user-controlled tensor, possibly empty or with fewer elements than restore_index

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: No such check exists in the vulnerable code. The code proceeds directly to index into the tensor without validating bounds.

relevant_state.value: attacker-controlled, e.g. 0 when tensor is empty, or beyond tensor size

step_by_step_execution.description: tensor_name_t.flat<tstring>()(restore_index) is accessed, causing OOB read or null pointer dereference

out_state.value: undefined - reads out of bounds or dereferences null

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the same vulnerability as CVE-2021-37639: missing bounds check on restore_index when indexing into tensor_name_t, leading to null pointer dereference (when empty) or heap OOB read. The fix adds exactly this bounds check (size > restore_index). CWE-125 (OOB read) is a valid characterization alongside CVE's CWE-476.

actual_issue_summary: Missing bounds validation on restore_index when accessing tensor_name_t. When tensor_name_t has fewer elements than restore_index+1 (or is empty), accessing tensor_name_t.flat<tstring>()(restore_index) causes a null pointer dereference or heap out-of-bounds read. The fix adds a check that tensor_name_t.NumElements() > restore_index before the access.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. PJSIP versions 2.12 and prior do not parse incoming RTCP feedback RPSI (Reference Picture Selection Indication) packet, but any app that directly uses pjmedia_rtcp_fb_parse_rpsi() will be affected. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

undesired_operation.description: Integer underflow in rpsi_bit_len calculation when attacker-controlled padlen exceeds rpsi_len*8 - 16, causing pj_strset to record a massive string length, leading to out-of-bounds read when the rpsi string is later consumed.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure via out-of-bounds read or denial of service via crash when the rpsi string (with a massive length) is subsequently accessed by the caller.

state.value: underflowed large value due to unsigned subtraction

step_by_step_execution.description: Header validation passes: pt==RTCP_PSFB and count==3
rpsi_len is computed from hdr->length. Attacker sets hdr->length such that rpsi_len is small (e.g., hdr->length=3 in network byte order gives rpsi_len=(3-2)*4=4)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Attacker provides a buffer of sufficient length (>=16), so this check passes.

step_by_step_execution.description: p is set to point past the header, padlen is read from the packet. Attacker sets padlen to a large value, e.g. 255.

in_state.value: points to byte after RTCP common header

step_by_step_execution.description: rpsi_bit_len is computed as rpsi_len*8 - 16 - padlen. With rpsi_len=4, this is 32 - 16 - 255 = underflow on unsigned type, yielding a huge value.

out_state.value: massive value due to unsigned underflow

step_by_step_execution.description: pj_strset sets rpsi->rpsi with a massive length derived from the underflowed rpsi_bit_len, enabling OOB read when the string is later accessed.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core vulnerability: missing validation of padlen relative to rpsi_len allows an integer underflow in rpsi_bit_len, leading to OOB read/write. The fix adds exactly the checks (padlen >= 32 and rpsi_len*8 < 16+padlen) that prevent this. This matches CVE-2022-24786.

actual_issue_summary: Missing validation of hdr->length (could be < 3 causing underflow in rpsi_len calculation) and padlen (could be >= 32 or cause rpsi_len*8 < 16+padlen) in pjmedia_rtcp_fb_parse_rpsi() leads to integer underflow in rpsi_bit_len and subsequent out-of-bounds read/write when the RPSI data is accessed.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseTensorSliceDataset` has an undefined behavior: under certain condition it can be made to dereference a `nullptr` value. The 3 input arguments to `SparseTensorSliceDataset` represent a sparse tensor. However, there are some preconditions that these arguments must satisfy but these are not validated in the implementation. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: Out-of-bounds read when accessing column 0 of a matrix with 0 columns. If indices has shape [N, 0] where N > 0 and values has shape [0], the loop iterates N times and calls indices->matrix<int64_t>()(i, 0), accessing a non-existent column.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read leading to information disclosure or denial of service via crash. An attacker can craft inputs with indices of shape [N, 0] to trigger this.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: [N, 0] is a valid matrix shape (2 dimensions), so the check passes

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Both are 0
Both are 0, so check passes
values shape [0] is a vector
dense_shape shape [0] is a vector
dim_size(0) is N > 0, so loop enters with i=0

step_by_step_execution.description: Access indices->matrix<int64_t>()(i, 0) on a matrix with 0 columns causes out-of-bounds read

judgments.reasoning: The finding identifies a specific path to undefined behavior due to missing validation of sparse tensor preconditions in SparseTensorSliceDataset. The CVE describes undefined behavior (nullptr dereference) from insufficient validation of sparse tensor inputs. The fix adds checks including dense_shape->NumElements() > 0 and dense_shape->dim_size(0) == indices->dim_size(1), which would prevent the scenario described. This is a valid manifestation of the same root cause (missing input validation).

actual_issue_summary: The SparseTensorSliceDataset implementation lacked proper validation of the relationship between its three sparse tensor input arguments (indices, values, dense_shape). Missing checks included: values count matching indices first dimension, dense_shape dimensions matching indices second dimension, and dense_shape having at least one element. This could lead to nullptr dereference and other undefined behavior when malformed inputs were provided.

cve_desc: This affects the package drogonframework/drogon before 1.7.5. The unsafe handling of file names during upload using HttpFile::save() method may enable attackers to write files to arbitrary locations outside the designated target folder.

undesired_operation.description: The member fileName_ (derived from an uploaded file's name, which is attacker-controlled) is appended to the destination path without any sanitization for path-traversal sequences. An attacker can set fileName_ to e.g. '../../etc/cron.d/evil' causing saveTo to write outside the intended directory.
undesired_operation.impact: Arbitrary file write — attacker can place files at arbitrary filesystem locations, potentially achieving remote code execution (e.g., writing cron jobs, SSH keys, or overwriting application files).

state.value: attacker-controlled string containing path traversal sequences like ../../etc/cron.d/evil

step_by_step_execution.description: fsPath is constructed from the user-provided path argument

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: 'uploads' is not absolute and has no parent path component starting with . or .., so the upload path is prepended

step_by_step_execution.description: fsPath is set to uploadPath / fsPath
fsFileName is constructed from the attacker-controlled fileName_ member without any sanitization
saveTo is called with fsPath / fsFileName, which resolves to a path outside the upload directory

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core vulnerability in CVE-2022-25297: the unsanitized fileName_ (from an uploaded file) is concatenated onto the destination path, allowing path traversal to write files to arbitrary locations. The fix removes the use of the member fileName_ in save() altogether and restructures the API. CWE-22 (Path Traversal) is closely related to the assigned CWE-552 (Files Accessible to External Parties).

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2022-25297: In HttpFileImpl::save(), the attacker-controlled fileName_ member variable (the uploaded file's original name) is appended to the destination path without sanitization for path traversal sequences (e.g., ../). This allows writing uploaded files to arbitrary locations outside the intended upload directory. The fix restructures the API so that the file name used for saving is explicitly provided by the caller rather than taken from the untrusted upload filename.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can cause undefined behavior via binding a reference to null pointer in `tf.raw_ops.RaggedTensorToVariant`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/ragged_tensor_to_variant_op.cc#L129) has an incomplete validation of the splits values, missing the case when the argument would be empty. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit be7a4de6adfbd303ce08be4332554dff70362612. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: When ragged_nested_splits_len is 0 and batched_input_ is true, batched_ragged_input.splits(0) performs an out-of-bounds access on an empty nested_splits vector.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential information disclosure from out-of-bounds read on an empty vector

The for loop over ragged_nested_splits_len does not execute since 0 < 0 is false, so nested_splits remains empty
