actual_issue_summary: When rt_nested_splits is an empty list and batched_input_ is true, the code accesses batched_ragged_input.splits(0) on an empty nested_splits vector, causing undefined behavior (out-of-bounds access). The fix adds a validation that ragged_nested_splits_len > 0 before reaching that code path.

cve_desc: scheme/webauthn.c in Glewlwyd SSO server through 2.5.3 has a buffer overflow during FIDO2 signature validation in webauthn registration.

undesired_operation.description: Multiple memcpy calls write attacker-controlled lengths of data into the fixed 200-byte stack buffer 'data_signed' without any bounds checking. The total written is 1 + rpid_hash_len + client_data_hash_len(32) + credential_id_len + 1 + cert_x_len + cert_y_len. If credential_id_len is large (e.g., 200), total exceeds 200 bytes, causing a stack buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow leading to potential arbitrary code execution. An attacker can craft a WebAuthn registration with a large credential_id to overflow the buffer and overwrite the return address or other stack data.

state.value: 1 + rpid_hash_len + 32 + credential_id_len + 1 + cert_x_len + cert_y_len, can exceed 200

rpid_hash is copied into data_signed at offset 1
client_data_hash (32 bytes) is copied into data_signed
credential_id is copied into data_signed with attacker-controlled length

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: With credential_id_len=200, offset would be 265, well beyond the 200-byte buffer. No bounds check exists, so memcpy writes past the buffer.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-40818: a buffer overflow in the fixed-size 200-byte stack buffer data_signed during FIDO2/WebAuthn signature validation. The fix replaced the stack buffer with a dynamically allocated buffer sized to the actual data. The CWEs (CWE-121/CWE-120 stack/buffer overflow) are consistent with the NVD-assigned CWE-787 (out-of-bounds write).

actual_issue_summary: The fixed-size 200-byte stack buffer 'data_signed' in check_attestation_fido_u2f could be overflowed when building the signature verification data, because the combined sizes of rpid_hash, client_data_hash, credential_id, cert_x, and cert_y could exceed 200 bytes. The fix replaces the stack buffer with a dynamically allocated buffer sized to exactly fit the data.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of SVDF in TFLite is [vulnerable to a null pointer error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/svdf.cc#L300-L313). The [`GetVariableInput` function](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L115-L119) can return a null pointer but `GetTensorData` assumes that the argument is always a valid tensor. Furthermore, because `GetVariableInput` calls [`GetMutableInput`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L82-L90) which might return `nullptr`, the `tensor->is_variable` expression can also trigger a null pointer exception. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5b048e87e4e55990dae6b547add4dae59f4e1c76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: tensor is dereferenced (tensor->is_variable) without a null check after GetMutableInput, which can return nullptr. This leads to a null pointer dereference.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash when GetMutableInput returns nullptr (e.g., invalid index)

step_by_step_execution.description: GetMutableInput is called and may return nullptr

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: There is no null check in the vulnerable code, so execution proceeds directly to dereferencing tensor->is_variable

step_by_step_execution.description: tensor->is_variable is evaluated, dereferencing a null pointer

out_state.value: crash / undefined behavior due to null pointer dereference

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the CVE-2021-37681 vulnerability: the missing null check on the return value of GetMutableInput before dereferencing tensor->is_variable. The CWE-476 (NULL Pointer Dereference) is the specific manifestation of the issue, consistent with the CVE's CWE-369 classification (the NVD CWE can differ). The fix adds exactly the null check this finding identifies as missing.

actual_issue_summary: GetVariableInput dereferences the pointer returned by GetMutableInput (via tensor->is_variable) without checking for nullptr. GetMutableInput can return nullptr for invalid inputs, causing a null pointer dereference. The fix adds a null check before the dereference.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the shape inference function for `Transpose` is vulnerable to a heap buffer overflow. This occurs whenever `perm` contains negative elements. The shape inference function does not validate that the indices in `perm` are all valid. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: c->Dim(input, in_idx) is called with a negative in_idx value. The check only validates in_idx >= rank but does not check in_idx < 0, allowing a negative index from attacker-controlled perm tensor to be used as a dimension index, leading to out-of-bounds memory access.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read — attacker can supply a negative value in the perm tensor to read memory before the dimensions array, potentially causing a crash (denial of service) or information disclosure.

state.value: negative value from attacker-controlled perm tensor

step_by_step_execution.description: rank is determined from input rank or perm elements
perm tensor data is read into data vector

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: in_idx is negative (e.g. -100), which is not >= rank (e.g. 3), so the bounds check passes

step_by_step_execution.description: c->Dim(input, in_idx) is called with negative in_idx, causing out-of-bounds access

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the CVE: the validation check only tests in_idx >= rank but not negative values, allowing negative perm elements to cause out-of-bounds access. The fix adds the check in_idx <= -rank to reject such values.

actual_issue_summary: The shape inference function for Transpose only checked if perm indices were >= rank but did not validate negative indices. Negative values in perm could bypass the bounds check and cause a heap buffer overflow/out-of-bounds read when used as an index into the input dimensions. The fix adds `in_idx <= -rank` to the validation check.

cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in 'int outlen = len * 4' when len > INT_MAX/4, leading to a small or negative outlen value passed to r_charset_encode_str while the actual buffer allocated by calloc(len, 4) is much larger.
code_snippets: int res = r_charset_encode_str (ch, out, outlen, buf, len);
undesired_operation.impact: Potential heap buffer overflow or incorrect buffer size tracking if r_charset_encode_str mishandles the negative outlen value, leading to possible code execution or crash when processing a crafted binary with RABIN2_CHARSET set.

initial_state.value: (st64)(to - from), validated to be >= 1 and <= ST32_MAX

step_by_step_execution.description: len is computed as (st64)(to - from) and validated to be between 1 and ST32_MAX

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: RABIN2_CHARSET environment variable is set to a valid charset

step_by_step_execution.description: outlen = len * 4 overflows int when len >= 0x20000000

out_state.value: negative or small positive due to int overflow

judgments.reasoning: The finding describes an integer overflow in the RABIN2_CHARSET code path (outlen = len * 4), but the actual CVE fix addresses a different integer overflow: needle + 4 could overflow ut64 near UT64_MAX, and the expression `buf + needle - from` had incorrect operator precedence causing out-of-bounds reads. The fix adds `needle < UT64_MAX - 4` to the while condition, changes `needle + 4 < to` to `needle < to - 4`, and adds parentheses to ensure correct arithmetic. The finding is about an unrelated code path that requires RABIN2_CHARSET to be set.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow when needle is near UT64_MAX causes the expression `needle + 4` to wrap around, and incorrect operator precedence in expressions like `buf + needle - from` (evaluated as `(buf + needle) - from` instead of `buf + (needle - from)`) leads to out-of-bounds reads in the string scanning loop. The fix adds an upper bound check on needle (`needle < UT64_MAX - 4`), rewrites `needle + 4 < to` as `needle < to - 4` to avoid overflow, and adds parentheses to ensure correct subtraction order.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.SparseReshape` can be made to trigger an integral division by 0 exception. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L176-L181) calls the reshaping functor whenever there is at least an index in the input but does not check that shape of the input or the target shape have both a non-zero number of elements. The [reshape functor](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L40-L78) blindly divides by the dimensions of the target shape. Hence, if this is not checked, code will result in a division by 0. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4923de56ec94fff7770df259ab7f2288a74feb41. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1 as this is the other affected version.

+            "Input tensor has ", nnz, " non zero elements but input shape (",
+            input_shape.DebugString(), ") or output shape (",
+            output_shape.DebugString(), ") is empty"));
     OP_REQUIRES_OK(context, functor::ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor<Device>()(
                                 context, input_shape, output_shape,
                                 input_indices_in.matrix<int64>(),

undesired_operation.description: Signed integer overflow in 'product *= size' when accumulating target shape dimensions.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via undefined behavior; potential out-of-bounds memory write if the compiler optimizes away downstream checks, leading to incorrect output shape computation and undersized buffer allocation.

In the loop, product *= size accumulates target shape dimensions

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2021-37640) is about division by zero in the ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor when the output shape has a zero dimension. The fix adds a check that dense_size > 0 && product > 0 before calling the functor. The finding describes a hypothetical integer overflow in product *= size, which is a different issue entirely and not what the CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: Division by zero in ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor when SparseReshape is called with nnz > 0 but the output shape contains a zero dimension (product == 0) or the input shape has zero elements (dense_size == 0). The fix adds a check that both dense_size > 0 and product > 0 before calling the reshape functor.

cve_desc: common/password.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because strncmp is used during hash comparison.

undesired_operation.description: strncmp is used to compare binary hash/key data. strncmp stops comparing at the first null byte ('\0'), so if both the computed hash (passwd1_sum) and the stored hash (passwd2_sum or key) contain a null byte at an early position, the comparison succeeds based on only a few bytes rather than the full hash length. This allows an attacker to bypass password authentication by finding a password whose hash shares only a short prefix with the stored hash (up to the first null byte).
undesired_operation.impact: Authentication bypass — an attacker can gain access by finding a password whose hash matches only a short prefix of the stored password hash up to the first null byte, drastically reducing the effective hash comparison length and making brute-force feasible. In the degenerate case where the stored hash starts with 0x00, approximately 1/256 of all passwords would bypass authentication.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-37848) is specifically about timing side-channel leakage: strncmp is non-constant-time, allowing an attacker to infer hash contents by measuring comparison time. The finding instead describes a null-byte truncation issue (CWE-187), which is a different vulnerability class. While both stem from misusing strncmp on binary data, the CVE description and CWE-200 clearly indicate the issue is timing information leakage, not partial comparison due to null bytes. The finding describes a different security consequence than what the CVE documents.

actual_issue_summary: Using strncmp (a non-constant-time string comparison function) to compare password hashes leaks timing information, enabling timing side-channel attacks. The fix replaces strncmp with crypto_memneq, which performs constant-time comparison.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.FractionalAvgPoolGrad` can be tricked into accessing data outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/fractional_avg_pool_op.cc#L205) does not validate that the input tensor is non-empty. Thus, code constructs an empty `EigenDoubleMatrixMap` and then accesses this buffer with indices that are outside of the empty area. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 0f931751fb20f565c4e94aa6df58d54a003cdb30. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

A negative or excessively large value leads to an out-of-bounds index into the in_backprop_tensor_temp buffer.
code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds heap read/write leading to arbitrary code execution or denial of service. An attacker who controls the row_seq_tensor or col_seq_tensor inputs can write to arbitrary memory locations relative to the in_backprop_tensor_temp buffer.

attacker-controlled, potentially negative or exceeding in_cols

attacker-controlled values accessible via flat indexing

in_col_start is read from col_seq_tensor_flat(c) without bounds checking
in_index is computed and used to access in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat out of bounds

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-37651) is specifically about missing validation that input tensor dimensions (in_batch, in_rows, in_cols, in_depth) are non-zero. When any dimension is 0, an empty EigenDoubleMatrixMap is created and then accessed with out-of-bounds indices. The fix adds checks that these dimensions are != 0. The finding instead describes a different issue: unchecked values in row_seq_tensor/col_seq_tensor leading to OOB access. While both relate to OOB access on the same buffer, they describe fundamentally different root causes. The finding does not identify the zero-dimension validation issue that is the actual CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The FractionalAvgPoolGrad implementation did not validate that input tensor dimensions (in_batch, in_rows, in_cols, in_depth from orig_input_tensor_shape) are non-zero. When any dimension is 0, an empty EigenDoubleMatrixMap is constructed, and subsequent accesses to it are out-of-bounds. The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checks ensuring all four dimensions are non-zero.

cve_desc: OwnTone (aka owntone-server) through 28.1 has a use-after-free in net_bind() in misc.c.

undesired_operation.description: After freeaddrinfo(servinfo) frees the entire addrinfo linked list, the code dereferences 'ptr' (which pointed into that freed list) via getsockname(fd, ptr->ai_addr, &ptr->ai_addrlen). This reads and writes to freed heap memory.
undesired_operation.impact: Use-after-free: getsockname writes into freed memory (ptr->ai_addr) and reads/writes ptr->ai_addrlen. This can cause heap corruption, crashes, or potentially arbitrary code execution if the freed memory has been reallocated.

Loop iterates through servinfo list, ptr ends up pointing to a successful entry, fd is a valid socket

in_state.value: points to a node in servinfo

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-38383: use-after-free in net_bind() where ptr is used after freeaddrinfo() has freed the memory it points to.

actual_issue_summary: After freeaddrinfo(servinfo) frees all addrinfo nodes, the code uses ptr (which points into the freed list) to call getsockname() and then net_port_get/net_address_get. The fix uses a local union net_sockaddr variable instead of the freed addrinfo memory.

cve_desc: Integer overflow in the VNC display driver in QEMU before 2.1.0 allows attachers to cause a denial of service (process crash) via a CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message, which triggers an infinite loop.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in '8 + dlen' where dlen is a uint32_t read from attacker-controlled data. When dlen is close to UINT32_MAX (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF), the addition wraps around to a small value (e.g., 7). This causes the caller to provide insufficient data, and client_cut_text is then called with an out-of-bounds pointer (data + 8) and an attacker-controlled length.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer over-read or potential heap buffer overflow via client_cut_text receiving a massive length value with insufficient backing buffer. Could lead to information disclosure, denial of service, or potentially code execution.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the root cause of CVE-2015-5239: an integer overflow in `8 + dlen` in the CLIENT_CUT_TEXT handler when dlen is a large attacker-controlled uint32_t value. The CVE describes this as an integer overflow that triggers an infinite loop (because the wrapped-around small return value causes the caller to repeatedly invoke this function without making progress). The finding identifies the same overflow, though it emphasizes buffer over-read consequences rather than the infinite loop. The fix addresses exactly this by rejecting dlen values over 1MB. The core vulnerability identified is the same.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in the VNC CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message handler: when a client sends a dlen close to UINT32_MAX, the expression `8 + dlen` wraps around to a small value, causing an infinite loop in the caller (which never accumulates enough data). The fix adds a 1MB limit on the payload size, rejecting oversized values with vnc_client_error().

cve_desc: njs through 0.7.0, used in NGINX, was discovered to contain a heap use-after-free in njs_await_fulfilled.

undesired_operation.description: When njs_vmcode_interpreter returns NJS_ERROR and the error is a memory error, the function returns NJS_ERROR immediately without calling njs_async_context_free(vm, ctx), leaking the async context memory.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service through memory exhaustion — repeated triggering of memory errors in async contexts causes unbounded memory leaks since the async context (ctx) is never freed.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE-2022-25139 is a heap use-after-free (CWE-416) caused by storing PromiseCapability in function->context, which gets overwritten during recursive async function calls. The fix passes capability and ctx as parameters to njs_vmcode_interpreter instead. The finding describes an unrelated potential memory leak in the memory error early-return path, which is not what the CVE is about.

actual_issue_summary: PromiseCapability record was stored directly in function->context during async function invocation. Recursive async calls would overwrite function->context, causing use-after-free when the original context was accessed. The fix passes capability and ctx as parameters to njs_vmcode_interpreter instead of storing them on the function object.

cve_desc: A memory leak in the rsi_send_beacon() function in drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_mgmt.c in the Linux kernel through 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering rsi_prepare_beacon() failures, aka CID-d563131ef23c.

undesired_operation.description: When rsi_prepare_beacon() fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the allocated skb, causing a memory leak.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via memory exhaustion — repeated beacon send failures leak MAX_MGMT_PKT_SIZE bytes each time

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2019-19071: a memory leak in rsi_send_beacon when rsi_prepare_beacon fails, due to missing dev_kfree_skb(skb) before the error return.

actual_issue_summary: In rsi_send_beacon(), when rsi_prepare_beacon() fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the previously allocated skb, causing a memory leak. The fix adds dev_kfree_skb(skb) before the error return.

cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.0.

undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (segmentation fault from writing to address derived from NULL pointer)

prelink_range is obtained from get_prelink_info_range_from_mach0

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions prior to and including 2.12.1 a stack buffer overflow vulnerability affects PJSIP users that use STUN in their applications, either by: setting a STUN server in their account/media config in PJSUA/PJSUA2 level, or directly using `pjlib-util/stun_simple` API. A patch is available in commit 450baca which should be included in the next release. There are no known workarounds for this issue.

undesired_operation.description: The while loop increments msg->attr_count without checking it against the bounds of the msg->attr[] array. Each iteration writes a pointer via *attr = (pjstun_attr_hdr*)p_attr where attr = &msg->attr[msg->attr_count]. A crafted STUN message with many small attributes can cause msg->attr_count to exceed the array size, resulting in an out-of-bounds write.
undesired_operation.impact: An attacker can send a crafted STUN message with many small (4-byte minimum after padding) attributes to write pointers beyond the msg->attr[] array, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution or crashing the application.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly identifies the CVE-2022-31031 vulnerability: the missing bounds check on msg->attr_count in the while loop leads to a stack buffer overflow when parsing STUN messages with many attributes. The fix adds exactly this check.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2022-31031 is a stack buffer overflow in pjstun_parse_msg where the while loop parsing STUN attributes does not check msg->attr_count against the fixed size of the msg->attr[] array. A crafted STUN message with many small attributes can overflow this array. The fix adds a bounds check (msg->attr_count < attr_max_cnt) to the loop condition.

cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11. This vulnerability is capable of arbitrary code execution.

undesired_operation.description: In the delete branch (mod=='d', dir=='<'), pos is decremented without checking if it's already 0. When pos becomes -1, decoded[pos] performs an out-of-bounds read before the buffer, and memmove writes to decoded + pos (i.e., decoded - 1), an out-of-bounds write.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read and write before the decoded buffer. An attacker who controls the rule input can read/write one byte before the buffer, potentially leading to information disclosure, heap corruption, or code execution.

initial_state.value: pointer to buffer of size INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX

step_by_step_execution.description: Rule byte sets mod='d' and dir='<' (e.g., c=3)
Rule byte in range (11-19) sets pos to 0 via pos -= (c - 10) when pos equals c-10, and dir=0
Rule byte sets dir='<' again (e.g., c=3 or c=4)
Next rule byte c > 20 triggers the else branch with mod=='d', dir=='<'. pos is decremented: pos--

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: In the vulnerable version, there is no check for pos < 0. The boundary check only tests l < 0 and d + l > decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX. With pos=-1, d = decoded + (-1) and l = *decoded_size - (-1) = *decoded_size + 1. d + l = decoded + *decoded_size, which may be within INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX, so the check passes.

step_by_step_execution.description: decoded[pos] is accessed with pos=-1, reading one byte before the buffer. Then memmove(d, s, l) writes to decoded-1, an out-of-bounds write.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the missing pos < 0 check in the delete branch, which is exactly what the fix addresses. The fix adds 'pos < 0' to the boundary checks in both insert and delete branches. This matches the CVE's description of a buffer over-read in the inflections parser with corrupt input.

actual_issue_summary: The inflections parser in mobi_decode_infl had wrong boundary checks allowing pos to become negative. The check for pos bounds was in the wrong place (only in the c>10&&c<20 branch), and the insert/delete branches lacked pos<0 checks. The fix moves the pos<0 check into both the insert and delete operation branches and also fixes the delete branch to check s+l instead of d+l for the upper bound.

undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash — an attacker who can influence host_number_string (e.g. through configuration) to cause expansion failure triggers a NULL pointer dereference

step_by_step_execution.description: expand_string is called on host_number_string and returns NULL
Ustrtol is called with NULL s, causing NULL pointer dereference

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential NULL pointer dereference in the host_number_string expansion code. The actual CVE-2010-4345 is about the configuration file permission check being bypassed when a non-default config file is specified via command line. The fix changes the condition from `!config_changed` to `trusted_config` so that file permissions are checked even for alternate config files when still running with root privileges. The NULL pointer issue is unrelated to this privilege escalation vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerable code uses `!config_changed` to decide whether to check config file permissions, which skips the check when an alternate config file is specified via command line (config_changed=TRUE). Since the exim user could specify an alternate config file containing arbitrary directives (like spool_directory with embedded commands) and the permission check was skipped, this allowed local privilege escalation. The fix changes the condition to `trusted_config`, ensuring permission checks are performed whenever the process retains root privileges.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a stack buffer overflow vulnerability that affects PJSUA2 users or users that call the API `pjmedia_sdp_print(), pjmedia_sdp_media_print()`. Applications that do not use PJSUA2 and do not directly call `pjmedia_sdp_print()` or `pjmedia_sdp_media_print()` should not be affected. A patch is available on the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

undesired_operation.description: The initial bounds check reserves only a fixed 24 bytes for all format entries combined, but the fmt loop writes (1 + fmt[i].slen) bytes per entry with no per-iteration bounds check. If the total size of format entries exceeds 24 bytes, the writes in the loop overflow past the end of the buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap or stack buffer overflow — an attacker who controls the SDP media descriptor (e.g. via a crafted SDP offer/answer) can write past the buffer boundary, potentially achieving remote code execution or denial of service.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core vulnerability: the fmt loop in print_media_desc writes format entries without per-iteration bounds checking, relying only on a fixed 24-byte allowance in the initial check. The fix adds exactly the bounds checks the finding describes are missing (checking end-p against fmt[i].slen each iteration). This matches CVE-2022-24764's stack buffer overflow in pjmedia_sdp_media_print().

actual_issue_summary: Stack buffer overflow in print_media_desc(): the loop writing SDP format entries and the subsequent \r\n writes had no bounds checking against the remaining buffer space. The fix adds per-iteration bounds checks in the fmt loop and before writing \r\n, returning -1 if insufficient space remains.

cve_desc: A memory leak in the fastrpc_dma_buf_attach() function in drivers/misc/fastrpc.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.9 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering dma_get_sgtable() failures, aka CID-fc739a058d99.

undesired_operation.description: When dma_get_sgtable() fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the previously kzalloc'd 'a', leaking kernel memory.
undesired_operation.impact: Kernel memory leak — repeated attach failures allow an attacker to exhaust kernel memory, causing denial of service.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: kzalloc succeeded, so a is non-NULL

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: dma_get_sgtable failed

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2019-19069: a memory leak of 'a' when dma_get_sgtable() fails, fixed by adding kfree(a) before the return.

actual_issue_summary: In fastrpc_dma_buf_attach(), when dma_get_sgtable() fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the kzalloc'd struct 'a', causing a memory leak. The fix adds kfree(a) before the error return.

cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in pcx_write_rle() in contrib/japanese/gdev10v.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

undesired_operation.description: After 'from += step', 'from' can be >= 'end', but the code dereferences '*from' in the condition 'data != *from || from == end' before checking whether 'from == end'. Since '||' evaluates left-to-right with short-circuit, '*from' is always read first, causing an out-of-bounds read when from >= end.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read past the buffer. Could cause a crash (denial of service) or information disclosure if the read value influences control flow (e.g., entering the else branch and encoding out-of-bounds data into the output file).

initial_state.value: pointer to start of input buffer
pointer to one-past-end of input buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: Read byte data from current position
Advance from by step

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: When from is near the end of the buffer, adding step can move from to end or past end

step_by_step_execution.description: Code evaluates 'data != *from' before checking 'from == end' due to left-to-right short-circuit evaluation of ||, causing out-of-bounds read

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact vulnerability fixed by the commit: the condition 'data != *from || from == end' dereferences *from before checking if from == end. The fix reorders to 'from >= end || data != *from' so the bounds check short-circuits first. While the finding uses CWE-125 (out-of-bounds read) and the CVE uses CWE-787 (out-of-bounds write), the underlying bug is identical — the condition ordering issue. The NVD describes it as a buffer overflow in pcx_write_rle() and the commit message confirms fixing the ordering in the if expression to avoid out-of-bounds access.

actual_issue_summary: In pcx_write_rle(), the condition 'data != *from || from == end' dereferences *from before checking whether from has reached or passed end. After 'from += step', from can be >= end, causing an out-of-bounds access. The fix reorders the condition to 'from >= end || data != *from' so the bounds check short-circuits before any dereference.

cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in the CalcMinMax function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick before 6.9.4-0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read and application crash) via a crafted mat file.

undesired_operation.description: ImportQuantumPixels reads past the end of heap-allocated BImgBuff because ldblk was computed via a 32-bit unsigned integer multiplication that overflowed, causing a much smaller buffer allocation than needed for image->columns pixels.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer over-read leading to potential information disclosure or crash (denial of service). An attacker crafts a MAT file with a large SizeX that causes the 32-bit multiplication to wrap, resulting in a tiny buffer that ImportQuantumPixels reads far beyond.

small value due to overflow wrap-around
heap buffer allocated with small ldblk * sizeof(double)

ldblk is computed as (4 * MATLAB_HDR.SizeX) with potential overflow
BImgBuff is allocated with overflowed ldblk
ImportQuantumPixels reads image->columns pixels worth of data from BImgBuff

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE fix adds `quantum_info=DestroyQuantumInfo(quantum_info)` after the main pixel-reading loop to properly clean up quantum_info before ExitLoop. The CVE is about an out-of-bounds read in CalcMinMax related to improper quantum_info lifecycle management, not about integer overflow in ldblk computation. The finding describes a different hypothetical vulnerability (integer overflow in buffer size calculation) that is unrelated to the actual fix applied.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability was caused by quantum_info not being destroyed (via DestroyQuantumInfo) after the main scanline reading loop before reaching ExitLoop. This missing cleanup led to heap-based out-of-bounds read conditions in CalcMinMax when processing crafted MAT files. The fix adds `quantum_info=DestroyQuantumInfo(quantum_info)` after the `do...while` loop completes.

cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in the client in HexChat 2.11.0 allows remote IRC servers to read or modify arbitrary files via a .. (dot dot) in the server name.

undesired_operation.description: strcat repeatedly appends recognized extension strings into a fixed 256-byte stack buffer without bounds checking. Since extensions_str is split by spaces and each token is checked independently, duplicate extension names in the input cause the same string to be appended multiple times, overflowing the buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow leading to potential remote code execution. An attacker (e.g., a malicious IRC server) controls extensions_str and can craft it with repeated recognized capability names to overflow the 256-byte stack buffer.

judgments.reasoning: CVE-2016-2087 is a directory traversal vulnerability (CWE-22) where a malicious IRC server uses '..' in the server name to read/modify arbitrary files. The finding describes a stack buffer overflow (CWE-120/CWE-787) due to unbounded strcat calls in CAP LS handling. While the commit does replace strcat with g_strlcat (fixing the overflow as part of cleanup), this is not the vulnerability described by CVE-2016-2087. The directory traversal and buffer overflow are entirely different issues.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2016-2087 is a directory traversal vulnerability where a malicious IRC server can use '..' sequences in the server name to read or modify arbitrary files on the client. The commit shown here cleans up CAP LS handling by replacing unsafe strcat with bounded g_strlcat and using a whitelist array for supported capabilities, but this is a code cleanup commit rather than the primary fix for the directory traversal CVE.

cve_desc: Use-After-Free in str_escape in mruby/mruby in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

undesired_operation.description: The local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized in the OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler block (L_SENDB_SYM). It is then used uninitialized in two places: (1) passed to prepare_missing() when the method is undefined, and (2) checked with mrb_proc_p(blk) and potentially dereferenced with mrb_proc_ptr(blk) after a C function method call returns. If stack garbage has a type tag matching MRB_TT_PROC, the code dereferences an arbitrary pointer from uninitialized memory.
code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);
undesired_operation.impact: Use of uninitialized stack memory can lead to information disclosure, crash (denial of service), or potentially arbitrary memory write (setting MRB_PROC_ORPHAN flag on an attacker-influenced garbage pointer) depending on the stack contents.

step_by_step_execution.description: blk is declared but not initialized
blk is used in prepare_missing or mrb_proc_p check

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about a use-after-free caused by creating a break object (via break_new) AFTER calling mrb_gc_arena_restore, which means the break object is not protected from GC. The fix moves break_new before mrb_gc_arena_restore. The finding describes an uninitialized variable issue with 'blk' in the SEND handler, which is a completely different issue unrelated to the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: In the OP_R_BREAK handler, when ci->cci > CINFO_NONE, break_new() was called after mrb_gc_arena_restore(), meaning the newly created break object was not protected by the GC arena and could be garbage collected, leading to a use-after-free. The fix moves break_new() before mrb_gc_arena_restore() so the object is created while still protected.

cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_java_bootstrap_methods_attr_new function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0. The bug causes the program reads data past the end 2f the intented buffer. Typically, this can allow attackers to read sensitive information from other memory locations or cause a crash. More details see [CWE-125: Out-of-bounds read](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/125.html).

undesired_operation.description: R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT reads 2 bytes from buffer at offset 6 (bytes 6 and 7) without checking that sz >= 8, causing an out-of-bounds read when sz < 8.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure or crash — attacker provides a crafted Java class file with a small attribute causing a heap/stack out-of-bounds read.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-1452. The fix adds a check `if (offset + 8 > sz)` before the R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT call at offset 6, preventing the out-of-bounds read when sz is too small. The finding correctly identifies the missing bounds check, the CWE-125, and the vulnerable code line.

actual_issue_summary: Missing bounds check in r_bin_java_bootstrap_methods_attr_new: R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT reads 2 bytes at offset 6 without verifying sz >= offset+8, causing an out-of-bounds read when a crafted class file has a bootstrap_methods attribute smaller than 8 bytes. The fix adds `if (offset + 8 > sz)` before the read.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in ncurses through v6.2-1. _nc_captoinfo in captoinfo.c has a heap-based buffer overflow.

undesired_operation.description: When sp points to '^' followed by a null terminator (end of string), the function increments sp past the null byte and still returns len=2. The caller, which uses the returned len to advance its pointer, will skip past the null terminator and begin reading out-of-bounds memory.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read in the caller — the returned len=2 causes the caller to advance past the null terminator, reading uninitialized or adjacent memory. This could lead to information disclosure or a crash.

This is exactly the bug fixed by the patch, which adds a check for c == '\0' and sets len=1 in that case. While the finding labels it CWE-125 (out-of-bounds read) and the CVE is CWE-787 (heap-based buffer overflow), the finding correctly identifies the same underlying defect — the missing end-of-string check leads to out-of-bounds memory access in the caller, which ultimately manifests as the heap-based buffer overflow described in the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: In cvtchar(), when processing a '^' character at the end of a string (followed by null terminator), the code did not check for end-of-string. It would read past the null byte and return len=2, causing the caller _nc_captoinfo to advance past the string boundary, leading to a heap-based buffer overflow.

cve_desc: Use after free in utf_ptr2char in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4646.

judgments.reasoning: The finding describes a potential out-of-bounds read due to unconditional pointer advancement in the MULTIBYTECODE case. The actual CVE-2022-1154 is a use-after-free in the RE_MARK case where getmark_buf() can free/reallocate the buffer line, causing rex.line and rex.input to become dangling pointers. The fix re-fetches the line after calling getmark_buf(). The finding describes a completely different issue unrelated to the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: In the RE_MARK case of regmatch(), calling getmark_buf() can cause the current buffer line to be freed and reallocated (e.g., due to autocommands). After this call, rex.line and rex.input become dangling pointers to freed memory. The fix saves the column offset before calling getmark_buf(), then re-fetches the line via reg_getline() and recomputes rex.input afterward.

cve_desc: An out-of-bounds write vulnerability exists in the drill format T-code tool number functionality of Gerbv 2.7.0, dev (commit b5f1eacd), and the forked version of Gerbv (commit 71493260). A specially-crafted drill file can lead to code execution. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

Execution continues to use tool_num as an index into image->aperture[], causing an out-of-bounds array read and potentially an out-of-bounds write when assigning a new aperture.

undesired_operation.description: In the TCS (Tool Change Stop) handling path, get_line(fd++) uses post-increment on the fd pointer, advancing it past the original gerb_file_t structure. After this call, all subsequent uses of fd (e.g., fd->filename) access memory beyond the valid structure, leading to out-of-bounds reads.

The CVE is specifically about the missing 'return -1' after the out-of-bounds tool_num check, allowing an out-of-bounds write via the aperture array. The fd++ issue, while potentially a real bug, is not what the CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a missing 'return -1' statement after detecting that tool_num is outside the valid range [TOOL_MIN, TOOL_MAX). The code logs an error but continues execution, using the out-of-bounds tool_num as an index into image->aperture[], leading to an out-of-bounds write (CWE-787). The fix adds 'return -1' to stop processing when an invalid tool number is detected.

cve_desc: Qt 5.x before 5.15.6 and 6.x through 6.1.2 has an out-of-bounds write in QOutlineMapper::convertPath (called from QRasterPaintEngine::fill and QPaintEngineEx::stroke).

The actual CVE is about a missing check for near-zero extent values that allows generation of huge numbers of tiny dashes, leading to an out-of-bounds write in QOutlineMapper::convertPath. The fix adds `qFuzzyIsNull(extent)` to the condition.

undesired_operation.description: In the while loop processing path elements with types, if *types does not match any known element type, the default case breaks without advancing pointers, causing an infinite loop.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via infinite loop.

The actual CVE is about missing the `qFuzzyIsNull(extent)` check, which allows a near-zero extent to bypass the dash simplification logic, leading to an out-of-bounds write from generating too many tiny dashes.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is an out-of-bounds write caused by generating a huge number of tiny dashes when the extent (visible area of the path) is near zero but not exactly zero. The fix adds `qFuzzyIsNull(extent)` to the condition that checks whether to approximate dashes with a solid line, catching pathological cases where extent is effectively zero but the ratio `extent / patternLength` doesn't exceed 10000.

cve_desc: drivers/bluetooth/virtio_bt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.3 has a memory leak (socket buffers have memory allocated but not freed).

undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service — a remote attacker (or malicious virtual device) can send packets with unrecognized pkt_type values, causing repeated sk_buff memory leaks that exhaust kernel memory.

skb_pull(skb, 1) advances the data pointer by 1 byte

The fix adds a default case with kfree_skb(skb). CWE-401 (missing free) is essentially equivalent to CWE-772 (missing release of resource).

cve_desc: A memory corruption issue was found in Artifex Ghostscript 9.50 and 9.52. Use of a non-standard PostScript operator can allow overriding of file access controls. The 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size resulted in a size that was too large, and could underflow to max uint32_t. This was fixed in commit 5d499272b95a6b890a1397e11d20937de000d31b.

undesired_operation.description: In backward search mode, the post-match string size is computed as 'count + size - 1', which is incorrect. This results in a string object whose size extends past the end of the source string's buffer, enabling out-of-bounds reads when the post-match string is subsequently accessed.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure — an attacker can read memory contents beyond the source string buffer by performing a backward search (e.g., via PostScript 'rsearch' operator) and then accessing the returned post-match string.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the root cause of CVE-2020-15900: the incorrect 'post' size calculation in the rsearch (backward search) path. The line `r_set_size(op1, count + (size - 1))` computes a size that is too large, and when size is 0 (empty pattern match), `count + 0 - 1` underflows a uint to max uint32_t, exactly as described in the CVE. The fix replaces this with the correct calculation `op[-3].tas.rsize -= count + size`. While the finding emphasizes out-of-bounds read (CWE-125) rather than the CVE's CWE-787 (out-of-bounds write), the underlying issue — incorrect size calculation (CWE-131) leading to memory corruption — is the same vulnerability. The finding also correctly identifies the specific problematic code line that was changed in the fix.

actual_issue_summary: The 'rsearch' operator's post-match string size was incorrectly calculated as `count + (size - 1)` in backward search mode. This gave a size that was too large, and when size=0 (empty pattern), the unsigned subtraction underflowed to UINT32_MAX, creating a string object with a massive size that enabled memory corruption via out-of-bounds access.

cve_desc: The ip6gre_err function in net/ipv6/ip6_gre.c in the Linux kernel allows remote attackers to have unspecified impact via vectors involving GRE flags in an IPv6 packet, which trigger an out-of-bounds access.

undesired_operation.description: Out-of-bounds read when dereferencing the GRE key. The expression `((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1` computes the key offset relative to `p` (which is at `skb->data + offset`), but `grehlen` already includes `offset`. This double-counts `offset`, causing the read to access `skb->data + 2*offset + 4` (without CSUM) instead of the intended `skb->data + offset + 4`. With a typical offset of 40 (sizeof(struct ipv6hdr)), the read is at byte 84 but pskb_may_pull only validated 48 bytes.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read from sk_buff data. An attacker sending a crafted ICMPv6 error for a GRE-over-IPv6 tunnel can read beyond validated packet data, potentially causing a crash (denial of service) or information disclosure of kernel memory.

p is set to skb->data + offset, flags is read from p[0]

step_by_step_execution.description: Key access expression: ((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1 = p + 12 - 1 = p + 11, which is skb->data + offset + 44 = skb->data + 84. But pskb_may_pull only validated 48 bytes, so byte 84 is out of bounds.

out_state.value: skb->data + 40 + (12-1)*4 = skb->data + 84, well beyond validated 48 bytes

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely identifies the CVE-2017-5897 vulnerability: the grehlen variable includes the IPv6 header offset, so using it to compute the key position relative to p (which already starts at skb->data + offset) double-counts the offset, causing an out-of-bounds read ~40 bytes past the intended location. This matches the commit message and fix exactly.

actual_issue_summary: In ip6gre_err(), grehlen included the IPv6 header offset, but was used to compute the GRE key position relative to pointer p which already pointed past the IPv6 header (at skb->data + offset). This double-counting of offset caused the key dereference expression `((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1` to read ~40 bytes beyond the actual GRE key location, resulting in an out-of-bounds read. The fix separates grehlen (now only GRE header length) from offset, and tracks the key position explicitly via key_off.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.16-rc6. There is a lack of check after calling vzalloc() and lack of free after allocation in drivers/media/test-drivers/vidtv/vidtv_s302m.c.

undesired_operation.description: vzalloc(VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ) can return NULL, but the return value is never checked. The encoder is returned with e->encoder_buf = NULL and e->encoder_buf_sz = VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ. Any subsequent use of e->encoder_buf (e.g., in vidtv_s302m_encode) will dereference a NULL pointer.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via kernel NULL pointer dereference when the encode function later attempts to write to the NULL encoder_buf

vzalloc(VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ) is called and returns NULL due to memory pressure

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The vulnerable code has no check on the return value of vzalloc, so execution continues unconditionally

step_by_step_execution.description: e->encoder_buf_sz is set to VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ despite encoder_buf being NULL
Function returns e with NULL encoder_buf; later use in vidtv_s302m_encode dereferences NULL

judgments.reasoning: This directly matches the CVE description: 'lack of check after calling vzalloc()' leading to NULL pointer dereference (CWE-476). The fix adds exactly this check.

undesired_operation.description: When kzalloc for ctx fails, the error path calls kfree(e) but does not free e->encoder_buf (allocated by vzalloc) or e->name (allocated by kstrdup). This leaks kernel memory.
undesired_operation.impact: Kernel memory leak of vzalloc'd encoder_buf and kstrdup'd name, which under repeated triggering could exhaust kernel memory leading to denial of service

kstrdup allocates e->name
vzalloc allocates e->encoder_buf successfully
kzalloc for ctx fails, returns NULL

judgments.reasoning: This matches the CVE description's 'lack of free after allocation'. The fix adds proper cleanup in error paths (out_kfree_buf frees encoder_buf, out_kfree_e frees name and e). This is a secondary component of the same CVE.

actual_issue_summary: vzalloc() for encoder_buf is not checked for NULL return, allowing a NULL pointer dereference later. Additionally, the error path when ctx allocation fails does not free encoder_buf or name, causing memory leaks. The fix adds a NULL check after vzalloc and proper cleanup via goto-based error handling.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in Poppler through 0.78.0. There is a divide-by-zero error in the function SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill at SplashOutputDev.cc.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in 'result_width = tBitmap->getWidth() * imgData.repeatX' and 'result_height = tBitmap->getHeight() * imgData.repeatY'. These are int*int multiplications that can overflow, producing small or negative values.
code_snippets: retValue = splash->drawImage(&tilingBitmapSrc, nullptr, &imgData, colorMode, true, result_width, result_height, matc, false, true) == splashOk;
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow via crafted PDF — drawImage allocates memory based on overflowed (small) dimensions while tilingBitmapSrc provides data based on actual (large) repeat counts, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

state.value: overflowed int value from tBitmap->getWidth() * imgData.repeatX
overflowed int value from tBitmap->getHeight() * imgData.repeatY

step_by_step_execution.description: The finding describes an integer overflow in multiplication, not a divide-by-zero

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2019-14494) is specifically about a divide-by-zero error when surface_width or surface_height is 0, and the code performs 'repeatX = result_width / surface_width' and 'repeatY = result_height / surface_height'. The fix adds a check for surface_width == 0 || surface_height == 0 before these divisions. The finding instead describes an integer overflow in a completely different part of the code (the multiplication of bitmap dimensions by repeat counts), which is unrelated to the actual CVE.

actual_issue_summary: Divide-by-zero in SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill when surface_width or surface_height is 0. The divisions 'repeatX = result_width / surface_width' and 'repeatY = result_height / surface_height' are performed without checking for zero denominators. The fix adds an early return when surface_width == 0 || surface_height == 0 before these divisions.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming RTCP BYE message contains a reason's length, this declared length is not checked against the actual received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP. A malicious actor can send a RTCP BYE message with an invalid reason length. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

undesired_operation.description: pj_memcpy reads reason.slen bytes from pkt+9, but reason.slen is derived from a length byte inside the packet (pkt[8]) and is only clamped against the destination buffer size, NOT against the actual available source data (size - 9). An attacker can send a small packet with a large length byte to cause an out-of-bounds read past the packet buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure of heap/stack contents adjacent to the packet buffer, or crash (denial of service) if the read hits unmapped memory.

state.value: value from pkt[8], potentially larger than (size - 9)
actual packet size, could be as small as 10

initial_state.value: pointer to incoming RTCP BYE packet data
actual size of the received packet (attacker-controlled, > 8)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Attacker sends a packet with size > 8 (e.g., size = 10) to enter the BYE reason parsing block.

step_by_step_execution.description: reason.slen is set to min(sizeof(peer_sdes_buf_), pkt[8]). The attacker sets pkt[8] to a large value, e.g. 200.
pj_memcpy copies reason.slen bytes from pkt+9 into peer_sdes_buf_. Only (size - 9) = 1 byte of valid data exists at pkt+9, but 200 bytes are read, causing out-of-bounds read.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-43804: the declared reason length from pkt[8] is not checked against the actual packet size, leading to an out-of-bounds read in pj_memcpy. The fix adds exactly the missing check (reason.slen = PJ_MIN(reason.slen, size-9)). CWE-125 matches as well.

actual_issue_summary: In parse_rtcp_bye, the BYE reason length read from the packet (pkt[8]) was only clamped against the destination buffer size but not against the actual received packet size, allowing an out-of-bounds read when a small RTCP BYE packet declares a large reason length. The fix adds an additional clamp: reason.slen = PJ_MIN(reason.slen, size-9).

cve_desc: off-by-one in io_uring module.

undesired_operation.description: Off-by-one error in bounds check: uses '>' instead of '>='. When fd == ctx->nr_user_files, the check 'fd > ctx->nr_user_files' is false, so execution continues. array_index_nospec then clamps fd to 0 (since fd is not < nr_user_files, mask becomes 0). This causes the function to read the file pointer from slot 0 and potentially cancel I/O for the wrong file descriptor instead of returning -EBADF.
undesired_operation.impact: When a user passes fd equal to nr_user_files (an invalid slot), the code does not return -EBADF. Instead, array_index_nospec clamps fd to 0, causing the cancellation to target the file at fixed slot 0 rather than the intended (non-existent) slot. This allows an attacker to cancel I/O operations belonging to a different fixed file slot than intended.

initial_state.value: user-supplied integer, can be any value including ctx->nr_user_files

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: cd->flags includes both flags, so the fixed file path is entered
When fd == ctx->nr_user_files (e.g., N), N > N is false, so the check does not return -EBADF. The correct check should be fd >= ctx->nr_user_files.

step_by_step_execution.description: array_index_nospec(fd, ctx->nr_user_files) clamps fd to 0 because fd is not strictly less than nr_user_files, causing the mask to become 0
io_fixed_file_slot reads from slot 0 instead of returning an error, and the file pointer from slot 0 is used for cancellation

out_state.value: file pointer from fixed slot 0 (wrong file)

judgments.reasoning: The finding precisely identifies the off-by-one error (CWE-193) that is the subject of CVE-2022-3103: using '>' instead of '>=' in the bounds check for fd against ctx->nr_user_files. The finding correctly notes that when fd equals nr_user_files, the invalid index passes the check and array_index_nospec clamps it to 0, causing wrong file targeting. This matches the exact fix in the commit.

actual_issue_summary: Off-by-one error in __io_sync_cancel: the bounds check used 'fd > ctx->nr_user_files' instead of 'fd >= ctx->nr_user_files', allowing fd equal to nr_user_files to pass validation. Since valid indices are 0 to nr_user_files-1, this permits an out-of-bounds access (mitigated by array_index_nospec clamping to 0, but still incorrect behavior).

cve_desc: An out-of-bounds access issue was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem. It could occur when the 'id->name' provided by the user did not end with '\0'. A privileged local user could pass a specially crafted name through ioctl() interface and crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

undesired_operation.description: The loop condition checks id->name[i] before checking i < SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN. If the name array is filled with non-null characters (not null-terminated), when i equals SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN, the expression id->name[i] is evaluated first, reading one byte past the end of the name array.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read of one byte past the name array within the snd_ctl_elem_id struct. This reads into the adjacent struct field (index), causing the hash to incorporate unintended data. In contexts where the struct has different layout or padding, this could leak information or cause inconsistent behavior.

initial_state.value: user-provided array of SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN bytes, all non-null (no null terminator)

step_by_step_execution.description: h is initialized from id->iface, id->device, id->subdevice
Loop iterates from i=0 through SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN-1, each time checking id->name[i] first (non-null) then i < SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN (true)

step_by_step_execution.branch_taken: true for the OOB read of id->name[SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN]
step_by_step_execution.reasoning: When i == SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN, id->name[i] is evaluated first due to C short-circuit evaluation order. This accesses one element beyond the name array bounds before the bounds check i < SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN can prevent it.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-3170: the loop condition order causes an out-of-bounds read of id->name[SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN] when the name is not null-terminated, because id->name[i] is checked before the bounds check. The fix reorders the conditions so the bounds check comes first.

actual_issue_summary: In get_ctl_id_hash(), the for-loop condition checks id->name[i] before checking i < SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN. Due to C's left-to-right short-circuit evaluation, when i reaches SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN with a non-null-terminated name, id->name[SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN] is read out-of-bounds before the index check can prevent it. The fix reorders the conditions so the bounds check comes first.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/netfilter/nf_tables_api.c in the Linux kernel before 5.19.6. A denial of service can occur upon binding to an already bound chain.

undesired_operation.description: chain->use++ increments the reference count without checking for arithmetic overflow. If an attacker can call this function enough times referencing the same chain (e.g., by creating many rules with NFT_JUMP/NFT_GOTO targeting the same chain), the u32 use counter wraps to 0. When the referencing rules are deleted, the use count underflows, allowing the chain to be freed while still referenced, leading to use-after-free.
undesired_operation.impact: Use-after-free leading to potential arbitrary code execution in kernel context. An attacker who overflows the reference count can cause a chain object to be freed while still in use by other rules.

step_by_step_execution.description: chain->use is incremented without overflow check

judgments.reasoning: The CVE-2022-39190 is about binding to an already-bound chain causing denial of service. The fix adds a `nft_chain_is_bound(chain)` check. The finding describes a hypothetical integer overflow of the use counter, which is an entirely different issue unrelated to this CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability allows binding to a chain that is already bound (has NFT_CHAIN_BOUND flag set), causing denial of service. The fix adds a check via `nft_chain_is_bound(chain)` that returns -EINVAL if the chain is already bound before allowing the use count increment and chain reference assignment.

cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

undesired_operation.description: strchr is called on drc_name which is not null-terminated after memcpy. If buf does not contain '\n' or '\0' within the first nbytes bytes, strchr will read past the copied data into uninitialized stack memory within drc_name, and potentially beyond the drc_name array boundary, constituting a stack buffer over-read.
undesired_operation.impact:
Information disclosure of uninitialized stack contents — the garbage data between the copied bytes and a found '
' or '\0' becomes part of the string passed to dlpar_add_slot. If no '
' or '\0' exists in the remaining stack memory, strchr reads out of bounds causing a potential crash (denial of service). Also, if strchr finds '
' in uninitialized stack memory beyond offset nbytes, end points there instead of &drc_name[nbytes], and the resulting string passed to dlpar_add_slot contains uninitialized stack data.

state.value: not null-terminated after memcpy; bytes beyond nbytes contain uninitialized stack data

initial_state.value: user-supplied input buffer from sysfs store
uninitialized stack buffer of MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN bytes

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: nbytes is less than MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN so we proceed past the check

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy copies nbytes bytes from buf into drc_name without null-terminating

out_state.value: first nbytes bytes contain user data; remaining bytes are uninitialized stack garbage

step_by_step_execution.description: strchr searches drc_name for '\n', but since drc_name is not null-terminated, it reads past nbytes into uninitialized stack memory

in_state.value: not null-terminated; garbage after nbytes offset may contain '\n'

out_state.value: points to a '\n' in uninitialized stack memory beyond nbytes, or NULL if none found before hitting '\0' in garbage

step_by_step_execution.condition: end != NULL (found '\n' in stack garbage beyond nbytes)
step_by_step_execution.reasoning: If uninitialized stack memory contains a '\n' byte, strchr returns a pointer to it. This is the dangerous path where the code writes '\0' to a location in the stack frame beyond the intended range.

relevant_state.value: points to stack memory beyond drc_name[nbytes-1]

step_by_step_execution.description: *end = '\0' writes a null byte into uninitialized stack memory where '\n' was found, potentially corrupting the stack frame. The resulting drc_name string contains garbage between the user data and this null terminator.

in_state.value: points to '\n' in stack garbage beyond the user-supplied data

out_state.value: contains user data + garbage + '\0', passed to dlpar_add_slot

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the core vulnerability: memcpy without null termination causes strchr to read uninitialized stack memory, leading to stack corruption when writing '\0' at the wrong location. This matches CVE-2021-28972, which describes the mishandled drc_name '\0' termination causing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame. The finding describes the read-side and write-side consequences of the same bug.

actual_issue_summary:
In add_slot_store (and remove_slot_store), memcpy copies user data without null-terminating the drc_name buffer. strchr then searches for '
' in uninitialized stack memory beyond the copied data. If a '
' byte happens to exist in the stack garbage, the code writes '\0' there, corrupting the stack. Additionally, the resulting drc_name string contains garbage data. The fix replaces memcpy with strscpy to ensure null termination, and only replaces '
' with '\0' if actually found (rather than unconditionally writing '\0').

cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

undesired_operation.description: When size > SSIZE_MAX, the assignment `left = size` causes signed integer overflow (implementation-defined, wraps to negative on two's complement). When `off` is nonzero, the negative `left` passes the `left <= 0` check, and `pipe->bufs[iter_head & p_mask].len += size` adds the enormous `size` value to the pipe buffer's len field, making it far exceed PAGE_SIZE.
undesired_operation.impact: Pipe buffer len field is set to a value far exceeding the actual page size, leading to out-of-bounds read/write when subsequent code processes the pipe buffer. Could lead to information disclosure or arbitrary code execution.

step_by_step_execution.description: left is assigned size, which overflows ssize_t

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: off is nonzero from data_start

step_by_step_execution.description: left -= PAGE_SIZE - off makes left more negative

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: left is negative due to overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: pipe->bufs[iter_head & p_mask].len += size adds enormous value

judgments.reasoning: CVE-2022-0847 (Dirty Pipe) is about the missing initialization of `buf->flags` in the pipe_buffer structure, which allows stale PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE flags to persist, enabling writes to page-cache-backed read-only files. The finding describes a hypothetical integer overflow issue with `size` and `left`, which is an entirely different problem unrelated to the actual CVE.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2022-0847 (Dirty Pipe): The `push_pipe` and `copy_page_to_iter_pipe` functions allocate new pipe_buffer entries without initializing the `flags` field. This allows stale `PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE` flags from previously used pipe buffers to persist, enabling an unprivileged user to write into page cache pages backed by read-only files, resulting in privilege escalation. The fix adds `buf->flags = 0;` to properly initialize the flags field.

cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the dynamicGetbuf function in gd_io_dp.c in the GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) through 2.2.3, as used in PHP before 5.6.28 and 7.x before 7.0.13, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (stack-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted imagecreatefromstring call.

undesired_operation.description: memcpy is called with rlen derived from a negative 'len' parameter. Since rlen is int but memcpy's size parameter is size_t, the negative value is implicitly cast to a very large unsigned value, causing a massive heap buffer over-read and write.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash, or potential information disclosure / code execution due to massive out-of-bounds read from dp->data and out-of-bounds write into buf.

judgments.reasoning: The finding incorrectly attributes the vulnerability to a negative 'len' parameter. The actual CVE (CVE-2016-8670) is about a negative 'remain' value (when dp->pos > dp->logicalSize). When remain is negative, the old code only checked 'remain == 0' and missed negative values, so rlen would be set to a negative remain, which when cast to size_t for memcpy becomes a huge value. The fix changes 'remain == 0' to 'remain <= 0' to catch negative remain. The finding identifies the correct dangerous memcpy call and the correct type of issue (integer signedness / buffer overflow), but misidentifies the root cause as negative 'len' rather than negative 'remain', which is a fundamentally different triggering condition not addressed by the fix.

actual_issue_summary: When dp->pos exceeds dp->logicalSize, the 'remain' variable becomes negative. The original code only checked 'remain == 0' to return EOF, so negative remain values passed through, setting rlen to a negative number. This negative rlen was then implicitly converted to a very large size_t when passed to memcpy, causing a stack-based buffer overflow. The fix changes the check to 'remain <= 0' to also catch negative values.

cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to a double free in bobj.c:rz_bin_reloc_storage_free() when freeing relocations generated from qnx binary plugin. A user opening a malicious qnx binary could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number a3d50c1ea185f3f642f2d8180715f82d98840784 contains a patch for this issue.

undesired_operation.description: If bf->o->bin_obj is NULL, then qo is NULL and qo->fixups dereferences a NULL pointer, causing a crash.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via NULL pointer dereference crash

step_by_step_execution.description: bf->o->bin_obj is assigned to qo
qo->fixups is dereferenced

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE-2022-36043 is a double-free vulnerability. `rz_list_clone` creates a shallow copy of the fixups list, so both the original and cloned lists contain pointers to the same RzBinReloc objects. When both lists are freed, those objects are freed twice. The fix replaces the shallow clone with a deep copy that allocates new RzBinReloc objects. The finding describes a hypothetical NULL pointer dereference, which is a completely different issue unrelated to the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: Double-free caused by `rz_list_clone(qo->fixups)` creating a shallow copy of the relocations list. Both the original list in QnxObj and the returned cloned list share the same RzBinReloc pointers, so when both are freed, each relocation object is freed twice. The fix creates deep copies of each RzBinReloc instead of using rz_list_clone.

cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0490.

undesired_operation.description: The locally allocated 'lookfor' pointer is never freed when exiting via 'goto returncmd' from the cmdline_browse_history() path, because c is a navigation key (K_UP/K_DOWN etc.) which is excluded from the VIM_CLEAR guard at the top of the loop.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via memory exhaustion — each time cmdline_browse_history returns GOTO_NORMAL_MODE (e.g., due to out-of-memory in history browsing), lookfor is leaked. Repeated invocations accumulate leaked memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: lookfor is set to a non-NULL value during history browsing

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: cmdline_browse_history returns GOTO_NORMAL_MODE

step_by_step_execution.description: goto returncmd is taken, skipping VIM_CLEAR(lookfor) at loop top

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential memory leak of 'lookfor', which is unrelated to CVE-2022-3235. The actual CVE is a use-after-free where b_im_ptr (pointing into a buffer's b_p_iminsert or b_p_imsearch) is dereferenced after the buffer may have been freed by a BufEnter autocommand triggered via cmdwin. The fix adds buf_valid() checks before dereferencing b_im_ptr. The finding describes a completely different issue (CWE-401 memory leak vs CWE-416 use-after-free).

actual_issue_summary: Use-after-free of b_im_ptr, which points to curbuf->b_p_iminsert or curbuf->b_p_imsearch. When a command window (cmdwin) is opened and a BufEnter autocommand causes the original buffer to be freed, b_im_ptr becomes a dangling pointer. It is then dereferenced in two places: (1) cmdline_toggle_langmap(b_im_ptr) when Ctrl_HAT is pressed, and (2) im_save_status(b_im_ptr) at function exit. The fix adds a b_im_ptr_buf variable to track which buffer b_im_ptr belongs to and uses buf_valid() to check the buffer is still valid before dereferencing.

cve_desc: A flaw was found in libvirt in the virConnectListAllNodeDevices API in versions before 7.0.0. It only affects hosts with a PCI device and driver that supports mediated devices (e.g., GRID driver). This flaw could be used by an unprivileged client with a read-only connection to crash the libvirt daemon by executing the 'nodedev-list' virsh command. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

undesired_operation.description: Due to C operator precedence, `*mdev_types[i]` is parsed as `*(mdev_types[i])` instead of the intended `(*mdev_types)[i]`. When i >= 1, `mdev_types[i]` reads memory at offset `i * sizeof(pointer)` past the `mdev_types` parameter (which points to a single pointer variable in the caller), producing an out-of-bounds read. That garbage pointer is then dereferenced and passed to virMediatedDeviceTypeFree, leading to use-after-free or arbitrary memory corruption.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read from the caller's stack followed by dereference of an arbitrary pointer value, leading to crash (denial of service) or potential code execution if attacker can influence adjacent stack memory.

state.value: pointer to a single pointer variable in the caller's stack frame

initial_state.value: pointer to caller's virMediatedDeviceTypePtr* variable (a single pointer)
pointer to caller's size_t count of existing mdev types
>= 2 (at least 2 existing mdev types to trigger the bug on second iteration)

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop variable i is initialized to 0

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: *nmdev_types >= 2 and i == 0, so loop body executes

step_by_step_execution.description: First iteration: *mdev_types[0] is equivalent to (*mdev_types)[0] due to pointer arithmetic coincidence (offset 0). This correctly frees the first element.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: *nmdev_types >= 2 and i == 1, so loop continues

step_by_step_execution.description: Second iteration: *mdev_types[1] is parsed as *(mdev_types[1]) due to operator precedence. mdev_types is a function parameter pointing to a single pointer in the caller. mdev_types[1] reads sizeof(pointer) bytes past mdev_types on the stack — an out-of-bounds read. The resulting garbage value is dereferenced and passed to virMediatedDeviceTypeFree, causing a segfault.
