step_by_step_execution.reasoning: DCHECK is no-op in release builds, execution continues with output_size[i] = 0

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the root cause of CVE-2022-21735: when pooling_ratio > input_size, output_size becomes 0, leading to division by zero (in GeneratePoolingSequence which divides by output_size) and potential crashes. The fix adds exactly the check this finding identifies as missing (input_size[i] >= pooling_ratio_[i]).

actual_issue_summary: In FractionalMaxPool, when pooling_ratio for a dimension exceeds the input size, output_size becomes 0 via floor(input_size/pooling_ratio). This causes a division by zero (likely in GeneratePoolingSequence or related computations). The fix adds an OP_REQUIRES check ensuring input_size[i] >= pooling_ratio_[i] for all dimensions.

cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.3 was discovered to contain a stack overflow in the function njs_default_module_loader at /src/njs/src/njs_module.c. NOTE: multiple third parties dispute this report, e.g., the behavior is only found in unreleased development code that was not part of the 0.7.2, 0.7.3, or 0.7.4 release

The bounds check therefore only validates dir->length (+ trail) against NJS_MAX_PATH, but the subsequent njs_cpymem copies both dir->length and info->name.length bytes into the stack buffer src[], potentially exceeding NJS_MAX_PATH + 1 bytes.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow — attacker who controls info->name.length can overwrite the return address and achieve arbitrary code execution.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact bug fixed in the commit: using assignment (=) instead of increment (+=) for length when dir is non-NULL causes the bounds check to only account for dir->length, not the total of dir->length + info->name.length. This allows a stack buffer overflow in src[], which is the CVE-2022-29379 vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: In njs_module_path(), when dir is non-NULL, 'length = dir->length' overwrites the previously stored info->name.length instead of adding to it ('length += dir->length'). This causes the bounds check against NJS_MAX_PATH to only validate dir->length, while the actual data written to the stack buffer src[] includes both dir->length and info->name.length bytes, leading to a stack buffer overflow.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can read from outside of bounds of heap allocated data by sending specially crafted illegal arguments to `tf.raw_ops.SdcaOptimizerV2`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/sdca_internal.cc#L320-L353) does not check that the length of `example_labels` is the same as the number of examples. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit a4e138660270e7599793fa438cd7b2fc2ce215a6. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: Out-of-bounds read on example_labels tensor: num_examples is derived from example_weights.size(), but example_labels.size() is never validated to be >= num_examples. The loop accesses example_labels(example_id) up to num_examples-1, which can read past the end of example_labels' backing buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash, or information disclosure of heap contents if the labels tensor is smaller than the weights tensor.

example_labels is read from context input but its size is not validated against num_examples

out_state.value: data read from beyond allocated buffer

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-37672: the missing validation that example_labels.size() == num_examples allows an out-of-bounds heap read. The fix adds precisely this check.

actual_issue_summary: The function Examples::Initialize does not validate that example_labels has the same number of elements as example_weights (num_examples). When example_labels is smaller, the loop reads out-of-bounds from the labels tensor's heap buffer. The fix adds a check that example_labels.size() == num_examples.

cve_desc: libjxl v0.3.7 is affected by a Divide By Zero in issue in lib/extras/codec_apng.cc jxl::DecodeImageAPNG(). When encoding a malicous APNG file using cjxl, an attacker can trigger a denial of service.

cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6.5 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component Item_args::walk_arg, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

undesired_operation.description: tdc_release_share(share) is called with an uninitialized 'share' pointer when the locked_tables_mode path reaches 'goto err_lock' via the vcol_fix_expr failure at the 'reset' label.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potentially arbitrary code execution. The uninitialized 'share' pointer will contain whatever stack garbage is present, and tdc_release_share will dereference it.

A matching table is found in the locked tables list

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the bug fixed by this specific commit. When the locked_tables_mode path is taken (goto reset), the 'share' variable is never initialized by tdc_acquire_share. If vcol_fix_expr then fails, the vulnerable code does 'goto err_lock' which calls tdc_release_share(share) on an uninitialized pointer. The fix changes this to DBUG_RETURN(true) to avoid the err_lock path. This commit is labeled 'MDEV-24176 fixup: GCC -Wmaybe-uninitialized' and is part of the fix chain for CVE-2022-27376 (MDEV-24176). While the root cause CVE is a use-after-free in Item_args::walk_arg related to virtual columns, this finding correctly identifies the specific bug that this commit addresses as part of the CVE remediation.

actual_issue_summary: The commit fixes a bug where 'goto err_lock' (which calls tdc_release_share(share)) was used when vcol_fix_expr() failed at the 'reset' label. When reaching 'reset' via the locked_tables_mode path, 'share' was never initialized by tdc_acquire_share(), making tdc_release_share operate on an uninitialized pointer. The fix replaces 'goto err_lock' with 'DBUG_RETURN(true)' to avoid calling tdc_release_share. This is a fixup commit for MDEV-24176, which is the main fix for CVE-2022-27376 (use-after-free in Item_args::walk_arg related to virtual column expressions).

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer has a use of unitialized variable. If the `train_nodes` vector (obtained from the saved model that gets optimized) does not contain a `Dequeue` node, then `dequeue_node` is left unitialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: dequeue_node is declared as an uninitialized pointer. If no train_node satisfies IsDequeueOp(), dequeue_node is never assigned, and the subsequent 'if (dequeue_node)' reads an uninitialized pointer, which is undefined behavior. If the uninitialized value happens to be non-null, the code dereferences it (dequeue_node->name()), leading to a crash or worse.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash, or potential code execution if uninitialized stack memory contains an attacker-influenced value that is dereferenced as a pointer.

state.value: uninitialized (no Dequeue node found in train_nodes)

initial_state.value: vector of NodeDef pointers from ComputeTransitiveFanin

out_state.value: uninitialized (contains whatever was on the stack)

Since dequeue_node is uninitialized, it may contain a non-null garbage value, causing the branch to be taken

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly matches CVE-2021-41225: the use of an uninitialized dequeue_node pointer in TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer. The fix initializes it to nullptr, which is exactly the one-line diff.

actual_issue_summary: In AutoParallel::Initialize, the pointer 'dequeue_node' is declared without initialization. If no Dequeue node exists in train_nodes, the pointer remains uninitialized and is subsequently read in a conditional check and potentially dereferenced, causing undefined behavior. The fix initializes it to nullptr.

cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository gpac/gpac prior to v2.1.0-DEV.

undesired_operation.description: If gf_sg_command_new returns NULL (e.g., memory allocation failure), com is NULL. gf_sg_command_field_new(com) is then called with NULL, and its return value inf is used without NULL check, leading to NULL pointer dereference at inf->new_node.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash due to NULL pointer dereference when memory allocation fails during parsing of attacker-controlled BIFS bitstream

step_by_step_execution.description: gf_sg_command_new fails and returns NULL
gf_sg_command_field_new called with NULL com
inf->new_node dereferences NULL pointer

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about Use After Free (CWE-416) related to improper node unregistration for USE nodes. The fix changes how gf_node_unregister is called for reused nodes to prevent freeing a node that is still referenced elsewhere. The finding describes a hypothetical NULL pointer dereference from allocation failure, which is a completely different issue unrelated to the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: Use After Free when a USE node (already registered elsewhere) that is not a QuantizationParameter is unconditionally unregistered via gf_node_unregister, potentially freeing memory still referenced by other parts of the scene graph. The fix conditionally unregisters only newly created nodes (num_instances == 0) and properly adjusts instance counts.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: Division by zero occurs when computing scales[i] because requested_min_max can be 0.0f when both input_requested_min_float and input_requested_max_float are 0.0f. This produces Inf/NaN scale values that are passed to the MKLDNN reorder primitive, potentially causing a crash or undefined behavior.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or undefined behavior when Inf/NaN scales are passed to MKLDNN reorder operation. An attacker can trigger this by providing input_requested_min and input_requested_max tensors with value 0.0f.

step_by_step_execution.description: input_requested_min_float is read from input tensor as 0.0f
input_requested_max_float is read from input tensor as 0.0f
requested_min_max = max(abs(0.0f), abs(0.0f)) = 0.0f
Division by requested_min_max (0.0f) produces Inf/NaN

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about incomplete validation of input tensor dimensions and sizes (e.g., missing checks that input_requested_min/max are scalars, and that validation of input dimensions happens before data is accessed, leading to null pointer dereference or OOB access). The finding describes a division-by-zero issue which is a different problem entirely and is not addressed by the fix. The fix reorganizes validation to happen before data access and adds checks for scalar inputs and tensor dimensions — it does not address the division by zero scenario.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerable function performs incomplete validation of input tensors in MklRequantizePerChannelOp. Specifically: (1) the input.dims()==4 check occurs after input_min_vec/input_max_vec data is already accessed, meaning a non-4D input could cause issues; (2) input_requested_min and input_requested_max are not validated to be scalars before accessing element (0), which could cause null pointer dereference or OOB access; (3) the DCHECK for input_requested_min_float < 0 is a debug-only assertion that doesn't protect in release builds. The fix moves validation before data access and adds scalar checks for requested min/max inputs.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a1bc56203f21a5a4995311825ffaba7a670d7747/tensorflow/core/kernels/sparse_reduce_op.cc#L217-L228) fails to validate that each reduction group does not overflow and that each corresponding index does not point to outside the bounds of the input tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 87158f43f05f2720a374f3e6d22a7aaa3a33f750. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: out_flat(idx) writes to an attacker-controlled index without bounds checking. The idx value is computed from sparse tensor coordinates (g.group()) which derive from user-supplied input_indices. If indices contain values inconsistent with (or exceeding) the declared input_shape, the computed flat index can exceed the allocated output tensor size.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds write on the output tensor buffer. An attacker can craft sparse tensor indices that are out-of-range for the declared shape, causing a write past the end of the allocated output buffer. This can lead to heap corruption, denial of service via crash, or potentially arbitrary code execution.

Output tensor is allocated based on reduction.reduced_shape

in_state.value: computed from shape and reduction axes

out_state.value: flat view of output tensor with finite size

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop iterates over groups from sparse tensor

out_state.value: may contain values outside valid range for the declared shape

step_by_step_execution.description: CoordinatesToFlatIndex computes idx from g.group() and output_strides without validation

out_state.value: attacker-controlled, can be negative or >= out_flat.size()

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: No such check exists in vulnerable code. idx is used directly without bounds validation.

step_by_step_execution.description: out_flat(idx) = reduced_val() writes to the computed index without bounds check

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly matches CVE-2021-37635. The CVE describes missing validation that indices don't point outside the bounds of the output tensor in sparse reduction ops. The fix adds exactly the bounds check on idx that this finding identifies as missing.

actual_issue_summary: In sparse reduction operations, the computed flat index (idx) from CoordinatesToFlatIndex is used to write to out_flat without checking that it falls within the allocated output tensor bounds. Crafted sparse tensor indices can produce an out-of-bounds idx, causing heap OOB read/write. The fix adds validation that g.group().size() matches output_strides.size() and that idx is within [0, out_flat.size()).

cve_desc: wolfSSL wolfMQTT 1.9 has a heap-based buffer overflow in MqttDecode_Disconnect (called from MqttClient_DecodePacket and MqttClient_WaitType).
diff:
--- vulnerable
+++ fixed
@@ -132,8 +132,9 @@
             /* Determine if we received data for this request */
             if ((wait_type == MQTT_PACKET_TYPE_ANY ||
                  wait_type == packet_type ||
-                 MqttIsPubRespPacket(packet_type) == MqttIsPubRespPacket(wait_type)) &&
-               (wait_packet_id == 0 || wait_packet_id == packet_id))
+                 (MqttIsPubRespPacket(packet_type) &&
+                  MqttIsPubRespPacket(wait_type))) &&
+                (wait_packet_id == 0 || wait_packet_id == packet_id))
             {
                 use_packet_obj = packet_obj;
                 waitMatchFound = 1;

undesired_operation.description: When re-entering the function in NONBLOCK mode with *mms_stat == MQTT_MSG_READ, packet_type is MQTT_PACKET_TYPE_RESERVED (0) and packet_id is 0, causing MqttClient_HandlePacket to be called with the wrong packet_type.
undesired_operation.impact: Type confusion: the packet handler processes continuation data using the wrong packet type, potentially misinterpreting packet data structures and causing memory corruption or incorrect protocol behavior.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2021-45936) is a heap-based buffer overflow caused by an incorrect matching condition: `MqttIsPubRespPacket(packet_type) == MqttIsPubRespPacket(wait_type)` incorrectly matches when both return false (0 == 0), causing a non-pub-response packet (e.g., DISCONNECT) to be processed using the caller's packet_obj instead of the generic client->msg object. The fix changes this to `MqttIsPubRespPacket(packet_type) && MqttIsPubRespPacket(wait_type)` so both must be true. The finding describes a completely different hypothetical scenario involving NONBLOCK re-entry and stale packet_type values, which is not what the CVE or the fix addresses.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability was caused by an incorrect boolean comparison `MqttIsPubRespPacket(packet_type) == MqttIsPubRespPacket(wait_type)` which evaluates to true when both functions return false (0 == 0). This caused non-pub-response packets (like DISCONNECT) to incorrectly match the wait condition and be decoded into the wrong packet object (packet_obj instead of client->msg), leading to a heap-based buffer overflow in MqttDecode_Disconnect. The fix changes the condition to require both to be true: `MqttIsPubRespPacket(packet_type) && MqttIsPubRespPacket(wait_type)`.

cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of making the radare2 crash, thus affecting the availability of the system.

undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (NULL pointer dereference) when processing a malformed NE binary

Code dereferences bin->segment_entries which is NULL: &bin->segment_entries[i] computes NULL + i*sizeof(NE_image_segment_entry), resulting in NULL pointer dereference

actual_issue_summary: NULL pointer dereference in r_bin_ne_get_segments when bin->segment_entries is NULL. The fix adds a NULL check for bin->segment_entries at function entry, returning NULL early if it is not set.

cve_desc: An unauthenticated stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in common.c's handle_PORT in uftpd FTP server versions 2.10 and earlier can be abused to cause a crash and could potentially lead to remote code execution.

undesired_operation.description: sprintf writes a formatted IP address string into the fixed-size stack buffer addr[INET_ADDRSTRLEN] (16 bytes). Since sscanf parses %d as full integers (not clamped to 0-255), attacker-controlled values like 2147483647 produce a string far exceeding 16 bytes, causing a stack buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow leading to potential arbitrary code execution or denial of service via crash. An attacker controlling the FTP PORT command argument controls the values of a, b, c, d.

state.value: attacker-controlled integers parsed by sscanf, can be any int value

step_by_step_execution.description: sscanf parses str into six integers a,b,c,d,e,f with no range validation
sprintf formats a,b,c,d into addr without bounds checking. The resulting string '2147483647.2147483647.2147483647.2147483647' is 43 bytes + null, far exceeding the 16-byte buffer.

judgments.reasoning: The finding precisely identifies the CVE-2020-20276 vulnerability: an unbounded sprintf into the stack buffer addr[INET_ADDRSTRLEN] in handle_PORT, which the fix replaces with snprintf(addr, sizeof(addr), ...). The CWEs (CWE-120/CWE-787) align with the NVD classification (CWE-787).

actual_issue_summary: In handle_PORT, sprintf is used to format attacker-controlled integers into a 16-byte stack buffer (addr[INET_ADDRSTRLEN]) without bounds checking. Since sscanf parses full integers (not byte-clamped), large values overflow the buffer. The fix replaces sprintf with snprintf bounded by sizeof(addr).

cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in 'len+2' passed to mrb_stack_extend. When len is a large mrb_int value (e.g., MRB_INT_MAX or MRB_INT_MAX-1), len+2 overflows to a small or negative value. mrb_stack_extend allocates insufficient stack space, and the subsequent copy loop writes len elements past the allocated buffer.
code_snippets: mrb_stack_extend(mrb, len+2); /* for receiver and (optional) block */
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow leading to potential arbitrary code execution or crash. An attacker who can control the number of arguments passed to fiber resume/transfer can trigger an out-of-bounds write on the fiber's stack.

state.value: MRB_INT_MAX or near MRB_INT_MAX such that len+2 overflows

initial_state.value: pointer to array of mrb_value arguments

step_by_step_execution.description: mrb_stack_extend is called with len+2 which overflows

out_state.value: small or negative value due to overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: Copy loop writes len elements starting from stbase+1

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE-2022-0890 is a NULL pointer dereference caused by not packing 15+ arguments into an array when switching to a newly created fiber. The mruby VM convention uses n=15 to indicate arguments are packed in an array, but the vulnerable code stored arguments individually on the stack and set n=len (which could be >=15), causing the VM to misinterpret the stack layout and dereference a NULL pointer. The finding describes a hypothetical integer overflow in len+2, which is a different issue entirely and not what this CVE is about.

actual_issue_summary: When a fiber is resumed/transferred with 15 or more arguments, the vulnerable code copies all arguments individually onto the stack and sets cibase->n to the raw argument count. The mruby VM uses n=15 as a sentinel meaning 'arguments are packed in an array,' so when n>=15 but arguments aren't packed, the VM misinterprets the stack layout, leading to a NULL pointer dereference. The fix packs 15+ arguments into an array and sets n=15 to match the VM's expectation.

cve_desc: The ReadMATImageV4 function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick 7.0.8-7 uses an uninitialized variable, leading to memory corruption.

undesired_operation.description: ReadBlob writes depth/8*image->columns bytes into the quantum pixel buffer, but the buffer was allocated by AcquireQuantumInfo before image->depth was updated. If SetQuantumDepth fails to reallocate (its return value is overwritten and never checked), the buffer remains sized for the original smaller depth, causing a heap buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow allowing potential arbitrary code execution via a crafted MAT v4 file. An attacker can craft a file where HDR.Type[1]==0 (depth=64), and if the quantum pixel buffer was allocated for a smaller depth (e.g. default 8 or 16), ReadBlob writes up to 8*image->columns bytes into a buffer sized for fewer bytes per pixel.

state.value: sized for original/default depth, smaller than 64-bit

step_by_step_execution.description: quantum_info is allocated via AcquireQuantumInfo with default depth
HDR.Type[1] is 0, so depth is set to 64
SetQuantumDepth is called but its return status is overwritten by SetQuantumEndian
ReadBlob writes depth/8*image->columns = 8*image->columns bytes into pixels buffer

in_state.value: quantum pixel buffer potentially sized for smaller depth

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2018-14551) is about the `status` variable being uninitialized before the while loop. The fix simply adds `status=MagickTrue;` initialization. If status is uninitialized and happens to contain MagickFalse, the function may incorrectly call DestroyImageList at the end, or conversely skip doing so when it should. The finding describes a completely different issue - a speculative heap buffer overflow from SetQuantumDepth failure - which is not what this CVE is about.

actual_issue_summary: The `status` variable in ReadMATImageV4 was not initialized before the while loop. At the end of the function, `status` is checked (`if (status == MagickFalse) return(DestroyImageList(image))`), but if the loop body never executes (e.g., immediate EOF), `status` contains an indeterminate value, leading to undefined behavior and potential memory corruption. The fix initializes `status=MagickTrue` before the loop.

cve_desc: Samba 3.6.6 through 3.6.23, 4.0.x before 4.0.18, and 4.1.x before 4.1.8, when a certain vfs shadow copy configuration is enabled, does not properly initialize the SRV_SNAPSHOT_ARRAY response field, which allows remote authenticated users to obtain potentially sensitive information from process memory via a (1) FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA or (2) FSCTL_SRV_ENUMERATE_SNAPSHOTS request.

undesired_operation.description: In the FSCTL_CREATE_OR_GET_OBJECT_ID case, a 64-byte buffer is allocated with talloc_array (which does not zero-initialize), but only 48 bytes are written (16+16+16). When max_out_len >= 64, *out_len is set to 64, causing the caller to return 64 bytes to the client, leaking 16 bytes of uninitialized heap memory.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure — 16 bytes of uninitialized heap memory (bytes 48-63) are returned to the client, potentially leaking sensitive data such as pointers, credentials, or other heap contents.

step_by_step_execution.description: *out_len is set to 64 since max_out_len >= 64
64-byte buffer allocated without zero-initialization
Only 48 bytes written to buffer (3 x 16 bytes)

out_state.value: initialized with file_id and volume objectid

judgments.reasoning: The finding describes an uninitialized memory issue in the FSCTL_CREATE_OR_GET_OBJECT_ID handler, but CVE-2014-0178 is specifically about the FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA handler where 4 extra bytes are added to the response buffer (`*out_len = 12 + labels_data_count + 4`) but never written, leaking uninitialized process memory. The finding identifies a different code path and a different issue entirely, not the CVE in question.

actual_issue_summary: In the FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA handler, `*out_len` was calculated as `12 + labels_data_count + 4`, adding 4 extra bytes beyond what was actually written. The buffer was allocated with talloc_zero_array (so initially zeroed), but the `needed_data_count` field was also incorrectly set to `labels_data_count + 4`. The fix removes the spurious `+ 4` from both the output length calculation and the needed_data_count field, preventing 4 extra bytes from being sent back to the client.

cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

undesired_operation.description: When instr[y].size is read from the file as a value less than 4 (e.g., 0, 1, 2, or 3), the expression 'instr[y].size - 4' underflows to a very large unsigned value. This value is passed as the length to f.read(), which writes into a 29-byte stack buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow leading to potential arbitrary code execution. An attacker can craft an XM file with a small instrument size field to trigger this overflow.

initial_state.value: unset, will be read from file

step_by_step_execution.description: header->ver is read and validated to be 0x104, entering the v0x104 instrument loading block
instr[y].size is read from the file via f.readDwords

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: With instr[y].size = 2, the condition is true, so we enter the block that reads into a stack buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: buffer[29] is allocated on the stack and zeroed. Then f.read(buffer, 1, instr[y].size - 4) is called. Since instr[y].size is an unsigned 32-bit value of 2, the subtraction 2-4 underflows to 0xFFFFFFFE.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches the CVE. The fix adds `instr[y].size >= 4` to the condition, preventing the unsigned underflow when size < 4. The finding correctly identifies the integer underflow leading to stack buffer overflow (CWE-787).

actual_issue_summary: When an XM instrument header claims a size less than 4, the expression `instr[y].size - 4` (unsigned) underflows to a huge value, causing f.read() to write far beyond the 29-byte stack buffer. The fix adds a check that `instr[y].size >= 4` before entering the small-header code path.

cve_desc: A double-free condition exists in contrib/shpsort.c of shapelib 1.5.0 and older releases. This issue may allow an attacker to cause a denial of service or have other unspecified impact via control over malloc.

undesired_operation.description: free(copy) is called a second time after it was already freed at the end of the for loop, resulting in a double-free
undesired_operation.impact: Double-free can corrupt heap metadata, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution or denial of service

step_by_step_execution.description: The for loop iterates over tokens, allocating result entries via realloc and dupstr

out_state.value: heap-allocated array of i string pointers

step_by_step_execution.description: After the for loop ends, free(copy) is called, releasing the copy buffer

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: At least one token was parsed in the for loop, so i >= 1

step_by_step_execution.description: realloc(result, sizeof *result * (i + 1)) is called to add space for a NULL terminator

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: realloc returned NULL, indicating allocation failure

step_by_step_execution.description: Error cleanup frees result entries and result, then calls free(copy) again on the already-freed pointer

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches CVE-2022-0699: the second error-handling path in the split() function calls free(copy) after copy was already freed earlier, causing a double-free. The diff confirms the fix removes this second free(copy).

actual_issue_summary: In contrib/shpsort.c's split() function, `copy` is freed after the for loop, but if realloc fails in the subsequent error-handling block, `copy` is freed again, causing a double-free (CWE-415). The fix removes the second free(copy) call.

cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

undesired_operation.description: vim_regsub_multi is called twice for each substitution: once with copy=FALSE to get the replacement length (sublen), and once with copy=TRUE to perform the actual write. When the substitution string uses \=expr, the expression is evaluated both times. If the expression is non-deterministic or has side effects, the second evaluation can return a longer string than the first, causing vim_regsub_multi to write more than sublen bytes into the buffer new_end, overflowing the heap-allocated new_start buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow. An attacker who can control the substitute command (e.g., via a malicious modeline, plugin, or sourced vimscript) can use a \= expression that returns a short string on the first (length-measuring) call and a long string on the second (copy) call, writing arbitrary data past the allocated buffer boundary, potentially achieving code execution.

initial_state.value: points to substitute text starting with \\=

step_by_step_execution.description: sub is set to point to the substitute string which starts with \= (expression substitution)

out_state.value: points to \\=expr (unchanged, no copy made in vulnerable version)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The substitute pattern starts with \=, so the regtilde branch is skipped in the vulnerable version (the negated condition is false)

step_by_step_execution.description: vim_regsub_multi is called with copy=FALSE to measure the substitution length

out_state.value: length returned by first expression evaluation

step_by_step_execution.description: Buffer new_start is allocated based on sublen

out_state.value: allocated heap buffer of size based on sublen

step_by_step_execution.description: vim_regsub_multi is called with copy=TRUE, evaluating the expression again. If expression has side effects or is non-deterministic, it may produce a longer result, writing past the buffer

judgments.reasoning: The CVE-2022-0413 is specifically a use-after-free: when `sub` points to `old_sub` and a recursive substitute command triggered by \= expression evaluation frees and reallocates `old_sub`, the `sub` pointer becomes dangling. The fix copies `sub` when it starts with \= to prevent UAF. The finding instead describes a heap buffer overflow from length mismatch between two vim_regsub_multi calls, which is a different vulnerability mechanism. While both involve \= expression evaluation with side effects, the finding misidentifies the root cause (TOCTOU on length vs. UAF of the sub pointer) and the CWE (CWE-787 vs. CWE-416).

actual_issue_summary: When the substitute pattern uses \= (expression evaluation), a recursive function call during evaluation (e.g., via `:s/^/\=execute("s#^##gn")`) can free `old_sub`, which `sub` points to. This causes use-after-free when `sub` is subsequently used. The fix makes a copy of the substitute text (`sub_copy`) when it starts with \= so it's independent of `old_sub`.

cve_desc: Integer overflow in the ljpeg_start function in dcraw 7.00 and earlier allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted image, which triggers a buffer overflow, related to the len variable.

undesired_operation.description: When tag is 0xffda and len is small, data[1+data[0]*2] and data[3+data[0]*2] access indices beyond the fread-populated portion of data, reading uninitialized stack memory.
undesired_operation.impact: Use of uninitialized stack memory — jh->psv and jh->bits get values from uninitialized portions of the stack buffer, potentially leaking information or causing incorrect behavior

judgments.reasoning: The CVE-2015-3885 is specifically about an integer overflow in the `len` variable. The fix changes `len` from `int` to `ushort` to prevent negative values when `(data[2] << 8 | data[3]) - 2` underflows (e.g., when both bytes are 0, producing -2 as int). A negative `len` passed to `fread` causes a buffer overflow. Finding 0 describes a completely different issue (out-of-bounds read via data[0] in the 0xffda case), which is unrelated to the len integer overflow vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in the `len` variable: when declared as `int`, the expression `(data[2] << 8 | data[3]) - 2` can produce a negative value (e.g., -2 when both bytes are 0), which when passed to `fread(data, 1, len, ifp)` causes a buffer overflow. The fix changes `len` from `int` to `ushort`, ensuring the value wraps to a positive unsigned value instead.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.19. In pxa3xx_gcu_write in drivers/video/fbdev/pxa3xx-gcu.c, the count parameter has a type conflict of size_t versus int, causing an integer overflow and bypassing the size check. After that, because it is used as the third argument to copy_from_user(), a heap overflow may occur. NOTE: the original discoverer disputes that the overflow can actually happen.

undesired_operation.description: On 64-bit systems, when count is large enough that count/4 exceeds INT_MAX, the truncation to int makes 'words' negative. This negative value bypasses the 'words >= PXA3XX_GCU_BATCH_WORDS' bounds check. Then 'words * 4' is passed to copy_from_user, where the negative int is implicitly converted to a very large unsigned long, causing a massive buffer overflow into buffer->ptr.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow leading to potential kernel code execution or denial of service. An attacker can write attacker-controlled data far beyond the bounds of buffer->ptr.

state.value: large value such that count/4 > INT_MAX (e.g., 0x200000004 on 64-bit)
very large unsigned value passed to copy_from_user

step_by_step_execution.description: count / 4 is computed as size_t (unsigned 64-bit), yielding 0x80000001, which is then truncated to int

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: words is negative (-2147483647), which is less than PXA3XX_GCU_BATCH_WORDS (a positive constant), so the size check is bypassed

step_by_step_execution.description: copy_from_user is called with words * 4 as the length. The negative int words * 4 is implicitly converted to a very large unsigned long.

out_state.value: very large unsigned long value, causing heap overflow

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely describes the CVE-2022-39842 vulnerability: the size_t to int type conflict in 'words' causes integer overflow, bypasses the bounds check, and leads to heap overflow via copy_from_user. The fix changes 'int words' to 'size_t words', which is exactly what the commit does.

actual_issue_summary: In pxa3xx_gcu_write, the 'count' parameter (size_t) is divided by 4 and stored into 'words' (int). On 64-bit systems, if count/4 exceeds INT_MAX, the truncation to int can produce a negative value that bypasses the size check, and when passed to copy_from_user causes a heap buffer overflow. The fix changes 'words' from int to size_t.

cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.2.9 has a Double Free via crafted USB device traffic (which may be remote via usbip or usbredir).

undesired_operation.description: adapter->rsi_dev is set to point to rsi_dev early in the function. In all error paths (fail_eps, fail_rx), rsi_dev is freed via kfree(rsi_dev) but adapter->rsi_dev is never set to NULL, leaving a dangling pointer. The caller or other USB subsystem code (via usb_set_intfdata) can then dereference adapter->rsi_dev, resulting in a use-after-free.
undesired_operation.impact: Use-after-free: after the function returns an error, adapter->rsi_dev points to freed memory. Any subsequent access to adapter->rsi_dev dereferences freed heap memory, potentially leading to code execution or crash.

judgments.reasoning: The finding identifies the same root cause as CVE-2019-15504: kfree(rsi_dev) in the error path while adapter->rsi_dev still holds a reference to it. The CVE describes this as a double free (CWE-415) because the caller rsi_probe invokes rsi_91x_deinit which frees adapter->rsi_dev again. The finding frames it as use-after-free/dangling pointer (CWE-416), which is the mechanism enabling the double free. Both point to the same problematic kfree(rsi_dev) that was removed in the fix. The finding correctly identifies the vulnerable code and the underlying problem.

actual_issue_summary: In rsi_init_usb_interface, on error paths rsi_dev (adapter->rsi_dev) is freed via kfree(rsi_dev). However, the caller rsi_probe also calls rsi_91x_deinit on error, which frees adapter->rsi_dev again, causing a double free. The fix removes the kfree(rsi_dev) from rsi_init_usb_interface's error paths, letting rsi_91x_deinit handle the cleanup.

cve_desc: Access of Memory Location Before Start of Buffer in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

undesired_operation.description: handle_subscript is called with name_start pointing to memory that was already freed via vim_free(alias). When alias is non-NULL, s is set to alias, then name_start is set to s (in the eval_variable path), then vim_free(alias) frees that memory, and then handle_subscript uses the dangling name_start pointer.
code_snippets: ret = handle_subscript(arg, name_start, rettv, evalarg, TRUE);
undesired_operation.impact: Use-after-free: handle_subscript reads from freed heap memory via name_start, potentially leading to code execution or crash depending on how handle_subscript uses the pointer.

step_by_step_execution.description: s is set to *arg
get_name_len returns with alias non-NULL, s is set to alias
name_start is set to s in the eval_variable path
vim_free(alias) frees the memory
handle_subscript called with dangling name_start

out_state.value: points to freed memory (used by handle_subscript)

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE-2022-0351 is about unbounded recursion in eval7() when processing expressions with many nested parentheses '(', causing a stack overflow crash (access of memory before start of buffer). The fix adds a recursion limit of 1000. The finding describes a completely different issue - a potential use-after-free with alias/name_start pointers - which is unrelated to the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is caused by unbounded recursion in the eval7() function when evaluating expressions with many nested parentheses '('. Each '(' triggers a recursive call through eval1(), and with enough nesting (e.g., 10000+ levels), the stack is exhausted, causing a crash with access to memory before the start of a buffer. The fix adds a static recursion counter limited to 1000 levels.

cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

undesired_operation.description: The nspace_declarations array is allocated with nspace_max_count = attribute_count + 1 entries, but for each attribute up to 2 entries can be written (one for namespace declaration, one for attribute value), causing a heap buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow allowing potential arbitrary code execution. An attacker who controls the XML element structure (attribute count and namespaces) can write past the allocated nspace_declarations buffer.

state.value: can exceed nspace_max_count when attributes have both namespace and value entries

step_by_step_execution.description: nspace_max_count is calculated as attribute_count + 1
nspace_declarations buffer is allocated with nspace_max_count entries

out_state.value: heap buffer of (N+1) * sizeof(struct nsd) bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: For each attribute i (0..N-1), up to 2 entries are written: one for namespace declaration and one for attribute+value via raptor_qname_format_as_xml

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: With N=3 attributes each having unique namespaces, nspace_declarations_count can reach 1 + 2*3 = 7, which exceeds nspace_max_count = 4, causing out-of-bounds writes

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely identifies the CVE-2017-18926 vulnerability: the miscalculation of nspace_max_count as attribute_count+1 instead of attribute_count*2 (+1 for element nspace), leading to heap buffer overflow when writing namespace declarations and attribute values.

actual_issue_summary: The nspace_max_count was calculated as attribute_count + 1, but each attribute can generate two entries in the nspace_declarations array (one for namespace declaration, one for attribute value). The fix changes the calculation to attribute_count * 2 and conditionally adds 1 for the element's own namespace, preventing heap buffer overflow.

cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

undesired_operation.description: When abbrev->count is 0, the expression 'abbrev->count - 1' underflows to SIZE_MAX (since size_t is unsigned), causing the loop to iterate an enormous number of times and access die->attr_values[i] and abbrev->defs[i] far out of bounds.
code_snippets: buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i], &die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read/write on die->attr_values and abbrev->defs arrays, leading to crash or potential code execution when processing a crafted DWARF binary with an abbreviation that has count=0.

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop condition evaluates abbrev->count - 1. Since abbrev->count is 0 and size_t is unsigned, this underflows to SIZE_MAX.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: i is 0 and abbrev->count - 1 is SIZE_MAX due to unsigned underflow, so 0 < SIZE_MAX is true

step_by_step_execution.description: memset writes to die->attr_values[i] which is out of bounds for large i values, and parse_attr_value accesses abbrev->defs[i] out of bounds

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-43814. The fix adds an `if (abbrev->count)` guard before the loop to prevent the unsigned integer underflow when abbrev->count is 0, which caused the heap-based out-of-bounds write described in the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: When abbrev->count is 0, the loop condition `i < abbrev->count - 1` causes an unsigned integer underflow (0 - 1 = SIZE_MAX for size_t), resulting in a massive loop that writes far out of bounds on die->attr_values and reads out of bounds on abbrev->defs. The fix adds a check `if (abbrev->count)` before entering the loop.

cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

undesired_operation.description: sprintf writes a PID string into a heap buffer starting at offset 7, but the buffer is only 14 bytes total (allocated by omStrDup of a 13-character string). A 7-digit PID (possible on 64-bit Linux where pid_max can be up to 4194304) requires 8 bytes (7 digits + null), overflowing the buffer by 1 byte.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow by 1 byte. Could corrupt heap metadata or adjacent heap data, potentially leading to code execution or crash.

initial_state.value: current process PID, potentially up to 4194304 on 64-bit Linux

step_by_step_execution.description: omStrDup allocates a copy of the 13-char string "/tmp/sd000000" plus null terminator = 14 bytes

sprintf writes PID as decimal string starting at filename+7. With a 7-digit PID like 4194304, it writes 7 chars + null = 8 bytes starting at offset 7, needing offsets 7-14, but buffer only has offsets 0-13.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE-2022-40299 is about using a predictable /tmp pathname (based on PID) that allows local users to perform symlink attacks and gain privileges of other users (CWE-269 / insecure temp file). The finding describes a potential heap buffer overflow from large PIDs, which is a different issue entirely. The fix replaced the predictable filename with mkstemp to create secure temporary files, not to fix a buffer overflow.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is the use of a predictable temporary file path in /tmp (constructed from the PID via sprintf). This allows local attackers to create symlinks at the predicted path before the victim process runs, enabling them to read/write arbitrary files with the victim's privileges. The fix replaces the predictable filename with mkstemp(), which creates a unique, unpredictable temporary file securely.

cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read in the BGP daemon of FRRouting FRR before 8.4 may lead to a segmentation fault and denial of service. This occurs in bgp_capability_msg_parse in bgpd/bgp_packet.c.

undesired_operation.description: memcpy copies sizeof(struct capability) bytes from pnt+1 into cap, but only 2 bytes after pnt are guaranteed to be within bounds (the check is pnt+3 > end, meaning only offsets pnt[0], pnt[1], pnt[2] are safe). sizeof(struct capability) includes at least code, length, and a union with mpc (afi: 2 bytes, safi: 1 byte), so it is larger than 2 bytes. This reads past the end of the input buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read from attacker-controlled network packet. Could cause information disclosure (heap/stack data leaked into cap struct) or crash (unmapped memory access). The read data in cap.length is then used to advance pnt, potentially causing further out-of-bounds access.

initial_state.value: pointer to start of capability message data from network
length of the capability message data

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: There is at least some data remaining in the buffer to parse
The buffer has at least 3 bytes remaining (action, code, length fields), so the check passes. But 3 bytes is less than sizeof(struct capability).

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy copies sizeof(struct capability) bytes from pnt+1 into cap. struct capability contains code (1 byte), length (1 byte), and a union (at least 3 bytes for mpc), so sizeof >= 5. Only 2 bytes from pnt+1 are guaranteed safe.

in_state.value: points to action byte in buffer

out_state.value: filled with data, some of which may be read out-of-bounds past end of buffer

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue of CVE-2022-37032: an out-of-bounds read in bgp_capability_msg_parse caused by memcpy reading sizeof(struct capability) bytes before validating the capability length field. The fix moves the memcpy after the length validation and only copies the smaller capability_mp_data structure. The CWE-125 (out-of-bounds read) matches exactly.

actual_issue_summary: In bgp_capability_msg_parse, a memcpy of sizeof(struct capability) bytes was performed from the input buffer before validating the capability's length field, causing an out-of-bounds read when the remaining buffer was smaller than sizeof(struct capability). The fix restructures the code to use a pointer cast for the header (avoiding the large memcpy), validates the length field first, and then copies only the smaller capability_mp_data after validation. Additionally, afi/safi validation was added via bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices to prevent out-of-bounds array access.

cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds write when getting data from PYC(python) files. A user opening a malicious PYC file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number 68948017423a12786704e54227b8b2f918c2fd27 contains a patch.

undesired_operation.description: When n1 is 0xFFFFFFFF (from a negative st32 value), malloc(n1 + 1) wraps to malloc(0), allocating a tiny buffer. Then rz_buf_read writes up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into it, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow leading to arbitrary code execution or crash. Attacker controls the data written via the buffer contents.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: For magic_int > 62061, get_st32 is called which can return a negative value cast to ut32

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: get_st32 successfully read -1, no error

rz_buf_read attempts to read 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into the tiny s1 buffer

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the integer overflow in n1+1 that the fix addresses with UT32_ADD_OVFCHK(n1, 1). This is the core vulnerability (OOB write from PYC files) described by CVE-2022-36040.

undesired_operation.description: When n2 is 0xFFFFFFFF (from a negative st32 value), malloc(n2 + 1) wraps to malloc(0), allocating a tiny buffer. Then rz_buf_read writes up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into it, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow leading to arbitrary code execution or crash. Attacker controls the data written via the buffer contents.

step_by_step_execution.description: n2 = get_st32(buffer, &error) returns -1 which becomes 0xFFFFFFFF as ut32

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: get_st32 successfully read -1, no error

rz_buf_read attempts to read 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into tiny s2 buffer

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the same class of vulnerability for n2, also fixed by adding UT32_ADD_OVFCHK(n2, 1). It's a second instance of the same OOB write vulnerability in CVE-2022-36040.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in get_complex_object when n1 or n2 is 0xFFFFFFFF (e.g., from a negative st32 read from a malicious PYC file). The expression n+1 wraps to 0, causing malloc(0) followed by a large rz_buf_read, resulting in an out-of-bounds heap write. The fix adds UT32_ADD_OVFCHK checks before the additions.

cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

undesired_operation.description: ReadOK reads up to 120 bytes (when biSize=124) or 104 bytes (when biSize=108) into a 64-byte stack buffer 'buffer[64]', causing a stack buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Arbitrary code execution via stack buffer overflow — attacker controls contents of a crafted BMP file that overwrites stack memory beyond the 64-byte buffer.

state.value: 104 or 120, both exceeding buffer size of 64

step_by_step_execution.description: File is opened and magic bytes are read and validated
12 bytes are read into buffer for the file header, then 4 bytes for biSize

out_state.value: attacker-controlled value from file, e.g. 108 or 124

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the root cause of CVE-2022-32323: the 64-byte stack buffer is too small for BMP v4/v5 headers (biSize 108 or 124), causing a buffer overflow when ReadOK writes up to 120 bytes. The fix increases the buffer to 128 bytes. While the CVE description says 'heap overflow', the actual fix and code clearly show a stack buffer overflow, and the finding accurately describes this.

actual_issue_summary: The stack buffer 'buffer[64]' in input_bmp_reader is too small. When reading BMP Version 4 (biSize=108) or Version 5 (biSize=124) headers, ReadOK reads biSize-4 bytes (104 or 120) into this 64-byte buffer, causing a buffer overflow. The fix increases the buffer size from 64 to 128 bytes.

cve_desc: In FreeRDP less than or equal to 2.0.0, an Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow exists. When using /video redirection, a manipulated server can instruct the client to allocate a buffer with a smaller size than requested due to an integer overflow in size calculation. With later messages, the server can manipulate the client to write data out of bound to the previously allocated buffer. This has been patched in 2.1.0.

Subsequent decoding into surfaceData will write far beyond the allocated buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow — attacker can supply large width/height values (e.g. via a crafted video presentation) causing a tiny allocation but full-size writes during video decoding, leading to remote code execution or crash.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in PresentationContext_new where 'width * height * 4' is computed as 32-bit arithmetic, causing BufferPool_Take to allocate a smaller-than-needed buffer. The fix computes the size using 64-bit arithmetic (4ULL) and rejects values exceeding INT32_MAX.

cve_desc: A memory leak in the cx23888_ir_probe() function in drivers/media/pci/cx23885/cx23888-ir.c in the Linux kernel through 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering kfifo_alloc() failures, aka CID-a7b2df76b42b.
